context engineering Write/Select/Compress/Isolate (prework 3.3)
CI/CD jako pierwsza produkcyjna pętla feedbacku (m1-l5)
`/10x-new` dla tworzenia change folder (m2-l1)
mustNotCover: tworzenie roadmapy i wybór slice'a (m2-l1)
planning pipeline: frame, research, plan creation (m2-l2)
interactive single-change implementation — `/10x-implement` (m2-l4)
interactive code review methodology — `/10x-impl-review` (m2-l3)
planowanie testów i quality gates (m3-l1)
pisanie testów i TDD (m3-l2)
team-shared agent infrastructure i shared AI registry (m5)
budowanie własnych serwerów MCP (future module)
debugowanie jako samodzielny temat (m3-l5)
learningOutcomes: Kursant konfiguruje parallel agent workflow: dwa worktrees, dwa agenty, dwie niezależne zmiany implementowane równolegle.
Kursant wybiera tryb pracy pasujący do zadania: interaktywny `/10x-implement` dla złożonych zmian, `/goal` lub headless `claude -p` dla prostych.
Kursant rozumie Ralph Wiggum loop jako uniwersalny wzorzec autonomicznej pętli i identyfikuje analogię w narzędziach spoza Claude Code.
Kursant orientuje się w ekosystemie narzędzi multi-session (Superset, Conductor, Antigravity, Agent View) i rozumie wspólny wzorzec: izoluj, deleguj, reviewuj.
Kursant doświadcza natural quality pain: "szybciej shipuję, ale skąd wiem, że to działa?" — rozumie, dlaczego M3 zaczyna od planowania testów.
videoPlaceholders: Guided first parallel run: prowadzący tworzy dwa worktrees, uruchamia `/goal` i headless `claude -p` na S-05 i S-06 (UX improvements), komentuje izolację kontekstu, wspomina Ralph Wiggum loop i Codex jako wzorce transferowalne.
Multi-session tools overview: prowadzący krótko pokazuje Superset/Conductor/Antigravity/Agent View jako narzędzia orkiestracji wielu sesji.
Quality pain bridge: prowadzący przegląda PRy i wyniki po kilku równoległych zmianach, zauważa brak testów i quality gates, komentuje przejście do M3.

groundingSources.title: 10x-auto-implement skill source
groundingSources.relevance: Canonical contract for /10x-auto-implement as a launcher that validates a plan, copies a separate-terminal command, and runs phases through fresh claude -p sessions without manual verification pauses.
claimsSupported: /10x-auto-implement accepts a change id or plan path and validates the plan before launch.
The skill copies a bash auto-implement.sh command for a separate terminal.
The launched script executes phases with fresh claude -p context and no manual verification pauses.
groundingSources.notes: Lesson should frame this as a launcher and deliberate downgrade from interactive /10x-implement, not as a correctness guarantee.

groundingSources.title: benihime auto-implement.sh
groundingSources.relevance: Implementation evidence for the actual headless script: per-phase claude -p calls, allowed tool list, max-turns support, checkbox-based resume, and stop-on-error behavior.
claimsSupported: auto-implement.sh calls claude -p once per phase.
The script passes an explicit allowed-tools list and --max-turns.
The script resumes by scanning unchecked plan progress rows and stops on failed phases.
groundingSources.notes: Internal local path; do not surface as learner-facing link.

groundingSources.title: Claude Code CLI reference
groundingSources.relevance: Current official reference for Claude Code print mode and automation-oriented CLI flags used by the headless implementation flow.
claimsSupported: claude --print / -p prints a response without interactive mode.
Print mode supports automation controls such as --max-turns, output formats, JSON schema output, no-session-persistence, and permission prompt tooling.
groundingSources.notes: Use for execution-mode facts only; it does not support claims about implementation quality.

groundingSources.title: Git worktree documentation
groundingSources.relevance: Official support for worktrees as multiple linked working trees attached to one repository, useful for isolating concurrent agent sessions.
claimsSupported: A Git repository can have one main worktree and zero or more linked worktrees.
git worktree add creates a linked working tree at a path and checks out a commit-ish.
Linked worktrees share repository data while keeping per-worktree state such as HEAD and index.
groundingSources.notes: Do not overclaim full runtime isolation; worktrees do not isolate ports, local databases, secrets, or external services.

groundingSources.title: 10x-impl-review-ci skill source and workflow template
groundingSources.relevance: Canonical contract for the non-interactive PR review workflow: plan discovery, seven review dimensions, committed report, inline and summary PR comments, and workflow-side verdict gate.
claimsSupported: /10x-impl-review-ci runs non-interactively in GitHub Actions and never asks questions.
The skill discovers the plan from a PR diff convention or Plan: override in the PR body.
The skill writes context/changes/<change-id>/reviews/impl-review.md, stamps change.md, commits the report with [skip ci], posts comments, and leaves the REJECTED gate to the workflow step.
CI review is a pre-scan and audit trail, not the interactive triage loop from m2-l3.
groundingSources.notes: Workflow template is same-repo and permission-sensitive; exact YAML should be rechecked before learner-facing copy.

groundingSources.title: Claude Code GitHub Actions
groundingSources.relevance: Official support that Claude Code can run inside GitHub Actions workflows and can be used for prompt-driven automation.
claimsSupported: Claude Code Action runs Claude Code inside GitHub Actions workflows.
The action can run prompt-driven automation and pass Claude CLI arguments.
Claude GitHub automation requires appropriate repository permissions when modifying files or responding on PRs/issues.
groundingSources.notes: Product details change; recheck before embedding exact configuration in the draft.

groundingSources.title: Workflow syntax for GitHub Actions
groundingSources.relevance: Official permission model for GITHUB_TOKEN scopes needed by a CI review workflow that writes commits, comments on PRs, and posts commit statuses.
claimsSupported: Workflow permissions can scope GITHUB_TOKEN access for contents, pull-requests, and statuses.
If any permission is specified, unspecified permissions default to none.
Fork PRs typically cannot receive write permissions unless repository settings explicitly allow it.
groundingSources.notes: Use to keep CI review setup permission-aware without turning m2-l5 into a GitHub Actions security lesson.

groundingSources.title: 10x-archive skill source
groundingSources.relevance: Canonical lifecycle-closure behavior for completed changes: move to archive, stamp frontmatter, warn on incomplete evidence, and commit locally.
claimsSupported: /10x-archive moves context/changes/<change-id>/ to context/archive/<created-date>-<change-id>/.
The skill stamps change.md with archived status, archived_at, and updated.
Uncommitted change-folder edits and pre-existing staged changes hard-block archive; other readiness issues are soft warnings.
groundingSources.notes: Frame archive as closing the workflow loop, not cosmetic cleanup.

groundingSources.title: Collaborator or Assistant? How AI Coding Agents Partition Work Across Pull Request Lifecycles
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Young Jo (seph) Chung, Safwat Hassan / arXiv
groundingSources.relevance: Current research signal for the lesson's review-capacity bottleneck: AI agents may initiate and carry PR work, while final merge governance remains predominantly human.
claimsSupported: In studied AI coding-agent PR workflows, operational agency and merge governance are decoupled.
Agent-initiated PR workflows can still retain terminal merge authority with humans.
groundingSources.notes: Very recent arXiv paper; useful for framing, but avoid presenting it as a universal empirical law.

newClaimsIntroduced: Goal-directed delegation (`/goal`, Ralph Wiggum loop, `claude -p`) jako autonomiczny tryb implementacji bez manualnych pauz.
Git worktrees jako mechanizm izolacji kodu i kontekstu dla parallel agent workflows.
Multi-session tools (Superset, Conductor, Antigravity, VS Code Agent View) jako ekosystem orkiestracji równoległych sesji.
Ralph Wiggum loop jako uniwersalny wzorzec autonomicznej pętli (Stop hook, while true, completion promise).
Dwa tryby pracy: interaktywny dla złożonych zmian, goal-directed dla prostych — wybór zależy od dojrzałości planu.
Complete M2 cycle (roadmap → plan → implement → review) jako repeatable workflow dla wielu slice'ów.
neighboringLessonReferencesChanged: m2-l3: bridge in — completed single-change cycle (m2-l1→m2-l3) jako prerequisite; m2-l5 nie powtarza interactive review.
m3-l1: bridge out — natural quality pain ('szybciej shipuję, ale skąd wiem, że to działa?') jako motywacja M3.
m1-l5: CI/CD z m1-l5 referenced jako infrastruktura pod CI review pipeline.
potentialDuplicates: Worktrees i parallel execution mogą zahaczyć o prework 2.4 (Agent-Native IDE). Granica: prework wspomina worktrees konceptualnie; m2-l5 uczy realnej orkiestracji z multiple agents.
Multi-session tools mogą zahaczyć o prework przegląd IDE. Granica: prework pokazuje co istnieje; m2-l5 uczy jak używać w kontekście parallel workflow.
unsupportedFacts: Learner-facing CLI ref (`m2l5`) i lista artefaktów lesson packa wymagają potwierdzenia w course-content wiring.
Dokładne URL-e produktów (Superset, Conductor, Antigravity) mogą się zmienić — zweryfikować przed publikacją.
needsHumanDecision: Czy S-04 i S-05 są wystarczająco gotowe oraz niezależne do parallel demo.
Czy video pokazuje dwa terminale split-screen live, czy jeden live + drugi jako podsumowanie.
Czy quality-pain bridge w demo wystarczająco motywuje przejście do M3.

lessons.title: Plan testów z AI: quality gates, test-plan i priorytety
lessons.titleEn: AI Test Plan: Quality Gates, Test-Plan, and Priorities
owns: /10x-test-plan jako kanoniczny learner-facing skill, który tworzy i utrzymuje context/foundation/test-plan.md oraz orkiestruje handoffy do cyklu M2 (/10x-new -> /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement) zamiast pisać testy w jednej sesji
context/foundation/test-plan.md jako trwały dokument-strategia + pamięć rollout'u, czytany przez skill przy każdym uruchomieniu; pliki na dysku, nie historia czatu, są źródłem stanu
Greenfield gate: jeśli projekt to PRD i notatki bez kodu, skill zatrzymuje się z komunikatem i odsyła do /10x-shape / /10x-prd, bo test-plan żywi się tym, co już istnieje w repo

Pozycjonowanie skilla jako warstwy orkiestracji nad cyklem M2, wizualizowane diagramem pętli (Test Plan -> kolejna faza -> /10x-new -> /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement -> aktualizacja planu)
risk-first prioritization: 5-7 failure scenarios w języku użytkownika/biznesu, ocenione impact x likelihood, z source traceability (PRD, roadmap, archive, hot-spot, interview)

Źródła sygnału: context/foundation/prd.md, context/foundation/roadmap.md, context/archive/*/plan.md, context/foundation/tech-stack.md, AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md, istniejąca konfiguracja testów, historia gita oraz wywiad z kursantem

Scope confirmation dla hot-spot scan: skill proponuje workspaces i wykluczenia (np. dist, coverage, fixtures, lockfile, snapshoty) i prosi o korektę zanim ruszy ze skanem, żeby churn lockfile'a nie zatopił prawdziwego sygnału

Adaptywny wywiad: skill dostosowuje pytania do stanu projektu - w greenfield pomija pytanie 'co czujesz, że jest niedotestowane', w sparse-suite projekcie pyta 'gdzie jest największa dziura, która cię niepokoi'
Wywiad-jako-źródło: obawy kursanta, miejsca wcześniejszych awarii i kod-bez-pewności są równoprawnym sygnałem ryzyka, nie miękkim dodatkiem do 'prawdziwej' analizy

Test-budget exclusions: ostatnie pytanie wywiadu ('na co NIE chciałbyś, żeby poszedł budżet testowy?') trafia do osobnej sekcji wykluczeń w planie z uzasadnieniem, żeby decyzja przetrwała rotację zespołu
Granica sygnał kontra wiedza: test-plan.md zostaje na poziomie failure scenarios i kategorii ryzyka; /10x-research dopiero ustala file:line, call graph i najtańszą warstwę testu

Problem wyroczni (oracle problem) jako rdzeń sekcji 'Zielony test, który niczego nie chroni': test musi znać oczekiwany wynik z niezależnego źródła (wymagania, kontrakt, zdrowy rozsądek), a nie z testowanej implementacji; przykład calculateDiscount + cztery linie obrony (przestrzeń na interpretację założeń, czytanie asercji zamiast paska pokrycia, sprawdzenie czy test potrafi zawieść - z zapowiedzią rozwinięcia w m3-l2, ostrożność wobec presji zielonego)

Aktywne pilnowanie granicy przez skill: ryzyko sformułowane implementacyjnie ('brak retry w session.ts') zostaje przeformułowane na user-felt scenario; nazwa pliku w kolumnie źródła zostaje zastąpiona numerem pytania z wywiadu, linią z PRD lub katalogiem z największym churnem

Dwukierunkowość rollout'u: kiedy /10x-research odkryje, że awaria mieszka w innym miejscu niż wskazywał hot-spot, kolejne uruchomienie /10x-test-plan proponuje korektę istniejącej fazy ('research wskazał poprawkę do §2, dopisać teraz czy zostawić do następnej rewizji?')
§6 Cookbook Patterns: placeholder 'TBD - see §3 Phase 1' na starcie; każda dowieziona faza dopisuje konkretny przepis (lokalizacja testów, polityka mockowania, test referencyjny, lokalna komenda)
Operational modes: /10x-test-plan --status (krótka tabela faz: zamknięte, w toku, następna komenda) i /10x-test-plan --refresh (świeża runda analizy, gdy plan się zestarzał)
7 pytań kontraktowych, na które context/foundation/test-plan.md musi odpowiedzieć po lekcji (ryzyka, źródła, profil testów, fazy, typy testów, rola AI w jakości, cookbook)
context/foundation/test-plan.md jako kontrakt wejściowy dla całego M3: m3-l2 startuje od pierwszej fazy rollout'u, m3-l3 / m3-l4 / m3-l5 implementują warstwy nazwane ogólnie w m3-l1
Repomix / GitIngest wzmiankowane wyłącznie w Materiałach dodatkowych jako narzędzia do pakowania repo jako briefu dla zewnętrznego LLM-a (nie sekcja Deep Dive, nie wbudowana ścieżka second-opinion)
referencesOnly: hierarchia rules-files: AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md, Cursor rules, memory, skille i prompt (m1-l4, prework 3.2) jako tło, ale test-plan.md nie jest tu domain-specific rules-file - jest osobnym żywym dokumentem w context/foundation
PRD jako źródło ryzyk biznesowych (m1-l1)
tech-stack.md jako wejście do rekomendowanego stacku testowego (m1-l2)
roadmap i zarchiwizowane slice'y jako wejście do likelihood i aktualnej powierzchni ryzyka (m2-l1, m2-l5)
CI/CD jako infrastruktura quality gates (m1-l5)
/10x-impl-review-ci jako jeden z automated review gates, nie substytut testów (m2-l5)
/10x-new, /10x-research, /10x-plan, /10x-implement jako downstream cykl, do którego /10x-test-plan przekazuje handoffy z STOP-em i instrukcją /clear (m2)
/10x-status jako miejsce, gdzie po M2 widać brakującą warstwę jakości i naturalne wejście w M3 (m2-l5)
Write/Select/Compress/Isolate z preworku 3.3 jako uzasadnienie świeżych sesji per handoff i trwałego planu w repo
prompt jako kontrakt z preworku 3.2 jako model mentalny dla context/foundation/test-plan.md
kryteria wyboru modeli z preworku 3.5 jako tło dla multimodalnego e2e
wymaganie testu i CI/CD z preworku 4.2 jako minimalny standard projektu kursowego
klasyczny Test Plan jako historyczny punkt odniesienia, nie osobny artefakt konkurujący z context/foundation/test-plan.md
mustNotCover: pisanie unit testów, konfiguracja Vitest, Testing Library, MSW albo mockowanie zależności (m3-l2)
konfiguracja hooków, hook lifecycle, debugging hooków i konkretne skrypty PostToolUse (m3-l3)
konfiguracja Playwright MCP lub innych MCP-ów, Playwright API, kod e2e i scenariusze multimodalne (m3-l4)
debugowanie od stack trace'a do fixa oraz pełny debug-as-test workflow (m3-l5)
research kodu na poziomie file:line, call graph, granic API i konkretnych modułów - to robi dopiero /10x-research w fazie rollout'u (m2)
pełny tutorial /10x-new, /10x-research, /10x-plan, /10x-implement - m3-l1 tylko pokazuje handoffy, cykl należy do m2
code review podczas implementacji slice'a i interactive triage findingów (m2-l3)
tworzenie CI/CD pipeline od zera, GitHub Actions YAML, sekrety i deployment config (m1-l5, m2-l5)
budowa AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md od zera lub pełna architektura reguł projektu (m1-l4)
wybór tech stacku aplikacji albo bootstrap projektu (m1-l2, m1-l3, prework 4.1)
performance/load testing jako osobna głębia poza krótką wzmianką w mapie ryzyk
security testing albo pen-testing jako osobny temat poza wpisaniem ryzyk security do risk map
budowa własnych MCP serverów do testowania
benchmarki modeli multimodalnych, porównania cenowe i rankingi narzędzi vision
sztywna rekomendacja Playwright MCP jako jedynego QA MCP; narzędzia mają być przykładami kategorii z datą checked
boilerplate template dla test-plan.md poza skillem - drafter nie powiela treści szablonu, tylko opisuje, co skill produkuje
learningOutcomes: Kursant uruchamia /10x-test-plan i rozumie, że wynikowy context/foundation/test-plan.md jest jednocześnie strategią, stanem rollout'u i cookbookiem, czytanym przez skill przy każdym uruchomieniu.
Kursant rozpoznaje greenfield gate: w projekcie bez kodu skill świadomie odsyła go do /10x-shape / /10x-prd, zamiast generować plan w próżni.

Kursant odróżnia risk map od listy testów: formułuje 5-7 failure scenarios w języku użytkownika/biznesu, koryguje impact x likelihood i wymaga source traceability (PRD, roadmap, archive, hot-spot, interview).

Kursant rozumie granicę sygnał kontra wiedza: zostawia file:line anchors dla /10x-research, a sam test-plan trzyma na poziomie failure scenarios; rozumie, że skill aktywnie przeformułowuje ryzyka opisane implementacyjnie i usuwa nazwy plików z kolumny źródła.

Kursant weryfikuje hot-spot scan jako likelihood evidence, potwierdza zakres skanowania (workspaces + wykluczenia takie jak dist/coverage/fixtures/lockfile/snapshoty) i nie traktuje hot-spotu jako dowodu na lokalizację buga.

Kursant odpowiada na adaptywny wywiad świadomie - własne obawy, miejsca wcześniejszych awarii i kod-bez-pewności traktuje jako równoprawne źródło ryzyka; ostatnie pytanie o test-budget exclusions traktuje jako kontrakt na przyszłość.
Kursant rozumie, że /10x-test-plan orkiestruje istniejący cykl M2 (/10x-new -> /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement) zamiast go zastępować, i wie, że pliki na dysku są pamięcią rollout'u.
Kursant przyjmuje dwukierunkowość plan-research: jeśli research wskaże, że awaria mieszka gdzie indziej niż hot-spot, plan przyjmuje korektę warstwy, która zna kod lepiej.

Kursant rozumie sekcję §6 Cookbook Patterns: na starcie placeholder (TBD - see §3 Phase 1), po dowiezionych fazach realne wzorce projektu (lokalizacja testów, polityka mockowania, test referencyjny, lokalna komenda).
Kursant wie, kiedy użyć /10x-test-plan --status (powrót po przerwie, krótka tabela faz) i /10x-test-plan --refresh (plan się zestarzał, nowa runda analizy).
Kursant wie, że m3-l2 startuje od pierwszej fazy rollout'u z context/foundation/test-plan.md, a kolejne lekcje M3 implementują warstwy nazwane w m3-l1 tylko ogólnie.
requiredFragments: Stała nowsza struktura workbench: intro bez `## Wstęp`, core content bez `## Core`, potem `## 🧑🏻‍💻 Zadania praktyczne`, `## Odbierz swoją odznakę`, `## 🔎 Deep Dive`, `## 📚 Materiały dodatkowe`.

Bridge-in z m2-l5: zarchiwizowane równoległe slice'y, szybszy throughput i pytanie 'skąd wiem, że to nadal działa?' jako naturalne otwarcie M3; jawna decyzja, że pierwsza lekcja M3 nie zaczyna się od `npx vitest`, tylko od pytania, co musi być chronione.
Konkretny failure mode: agent generuje wiele testów na helpers/getters/formatery, coverage rośnie, a auth/płatności/krytyczny flow użytkownika zostają bez ochrony.

Jawne pozycjonowanie `/10x-test-plan` jako warstwy orkiestracji nad cyklem M2: skill ustala, co chronić najpierw, a `/10x-research`, `/10x-plan`, `/10x-implement` dowożą; w lekcji znajduje się diagram pętli (Test Plan -> kolejna faza -> /10x-new -> /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement -> aktualizacja planu).

Wyjaśnienie różnicy między klasycznym, jednorazowym Test Planem a `context/foundation/test-plan.md` jako żywym dokumentem-strategią + pamięcią rollout'u, czytanym przez skill przy każdym uruchomieniu; jawne stwierdzenie, że pliki na dysku, nie historia czatu, są źródłem stanu.
Greenfield gate: krótkie zastrzeżenie, że jeśli projekt to dopiero PRD i kilka notatek bez kodu, skill zatrzyma się z komunikatem i odeśle do `/10x-shape` i `/10x-prd`.

Wejścia skillu wymienione w prozie: `context/foundation/prd.md`, `context/foundation/roadmap.md`, `context/archive/*/plan.md`, `context/foundation/tech-stack.md`, `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md`, istniejąca konfiguracja testów oraz historia gita.

Scope confirmation dla hot-spot scan z konkretnym przykładem (np. 'wykryłem packages/api/src, packages/course-content/src, wykluczyłem dist, coverage, fixtures') i ostrzeżeniem, że skan rozciągnięty na lockfile albo snapshoty zatopi prawdziwy sygnał.

Adaptywny wywiad: skill dostosowuje pytania do stanu projektu - greenfield bez pytania 'co czujesz, że jest niedotestowane', sparse-suite z pytaniem 'gdzie jest największa dziura, która cię niepokoi'.
Wywiad-jako-źródło: obawy kursanta, miejsca wcześniejszych awarii i kod-bez-pewności jako równoprawne źródło ryzyka, nie miękki dodatek; jawne nazwanie wywiadu obok PRD/roadmapy/archive/hot-spotów.

Test-budget exclusions jako wyróżniony moment: ostatnie pytanie wywiadu 'na co NIE chciałbyś, żeby poszedł budżet testowy?' trafia do osobnej sekcji wykluczeń w planie, z uzasadnieniem dla przyszłego czytelnika.
Risk map jako mapa scenariuszy ocenionych przez impact x likelihood, ze źródłami w prozie (PRD, roadmap, archive, hot-spot, interview).
Granica sygnał kontra wiedza explicit: test-plan.md trzyma ryzyko na poziomie scenariuszy, hot-spot to likelihood, nie file:line; `/10x-research` dopiero ustala realne miejsca w kodzie.

Aktywne egzekwowanie granicy przez skill: ryzyko w stylu 'brak retry w session.ts' zostaje przeformułowane na user-felt scenario, a nazwa pliku w kolumnie źródła zostaje zastąpiona numerem pytania z wywiadu, linią z PRD lub katalogiem z największym churnem.

Dwukierunkowość plan-research: jeśli research odkryje, że awaria mieszka gdzie indziej niż wskazywał hot-spot, kolejne uruchomienie `/10x-test-plan` proponuje korektę istniejącej fazy ('research wskazał poprawkę do §2, dopisać teraz czy zostawić do następnej rewizji?').

Czterostopniowy cykl po wyłonieniu ryzyka opisany jako: 1) `/10x-research` sprawdza, gdzie ryzyko naprawdę występuje; 2) `/10x-plan` zamienia research w małe fazy; 3) `/10x-implement` dowozi jedną fazę naraz; 4) ostatnia faza aktualizuje `test-plan.md` o cookbook.

Sekcja §6 Cookbook Patterns: na starcie placeholder ('TBD - see §3 Phase 1'), po dowiezionej fazie konkretny przepis (lokalizacja, polityka mockowania, test referencyjny, komenda lokalna); jawna decyzja, że skill nie udaje znajomości lokalnych wzorców przed researchem.
Operational modes: `/10x-test-plan --status` (krótka tabela faz: zamknięte, w toku, następna komenda) i `/10x-test-plan --refresh` (świeża runda analizy, gdy plan się zestarzał).
7 pytań kontraktowych, na które `context/foundation/test-plan.md` musi odpowiedzieć po lekcji (ryzyka, źródła, profil testów, fazy procesu QA, typy testów, rola AI w jakości, cookbook).
Deep Dive: ograniczone do dwóch sekcji - risk-based testing jako klasyczna dyscyplina (ISTQB) oraz sygnał kontra wiedza jako rozróżnienie między ryzykiem biznesowym a przyczyną techniczną.
Zadania praktyczne: uruchom `/10x-test-plan`, obroń mapę ryzyk (istotność, źródła, brak file:line), sprawdź dopasowanie planu do projektu (sekcje Stack i Quality Gates).
Odbierz odznakę tylko po realnym `context/foundation/test-plan.md` ze zaakceptowaną mapą ryzyk i opisanymi fazami rollout'u (bez wymagania pełnej tabeli quality gates ani gotowego cookbooka).
Materiały dodatkowe: ISTQB risk-based testing, Repomix, GitIngest, prework 1.3 / 3.2 / 3.3.
videoPlaceholders: Lekcja używa jednego osadzonego nagrania (Vimeo iframe) na końcu sekcji 'Sygnał, nie diagnoza', przed sekcją 'Cookbook zamiast pamięci w głowie'. Scenariusz pokazuje całą koncepcję posługiwania się `/10x-test-plan` w jednej spójnej demonstracji.
Bridge-in: `/10x-status` po M2, zarchiwizowane slice'y, brak testów - naturalne wejście w M3.

Aktywacja: `/10x-test-plan` startuje, agent czyta artefakty z `context/foundation/` + `context/archive/` + AGENTS.md, proponuje zakres hot-spot scan z konkretnymi workspaces i wykluczeniami (np. dist, coverage, fixtures); prowadzący koryguje zakres.
Wywiad: skill zadaje pytania dopasowane do stanu projektu, kończąc na pytaniu o test-budget exclusions, które trafia do osobnej sekcji planu.

Risk map: prowadzący próbuje wpisać ryzyko sformułowane implementacyjnie ('brak retry w session.ts') i widzi, jak skill przeformułowuje je na user-felt scenario; evidence w kolumnie źródła nie zawiera file:line, tylko numer pytania z wywiadu, linię z PRD lub katalog z największym churnem.
Cookbook preview: prowadzący ogląda placeholder w §6 ('TBD - see §3 Phase 1') i krótkie wyjaśnienie, dlaczego skill nie udaje znajomości lokalnych wzorców przed researchem.
Pierwszy handoff: skill kieruje do cyklu M2 (`/10x-new`), bez wchodzenia w sam cykl - to terytorium M2 i kolejnych lekcji M3.
Powrót: `/10x-test-plan --status` raportuje stan po przerwie w postaci krótkiej tabeli faz.

groundingSources.title: Playwright MCP server (microsoft/playwright-mcp)
groundingSources.relevance: Canonical example of a QA-oriented MCP server: structured accessibility snapshots, 15+ MCP clients, active maintenance. Lets the lesson name a concrete current MCP for browser control while keeping the recommendation category-level.
claimsSupported: Playwright MCP is a stable, named example of a QA-oriented MCP server in 2026-05.
Accessibility-tree-based browser automation is more deterministic for agents than vision-only approaches.
groundingSources.notes: Lesson must frame Playwright MCP as 'the most prominent current example,' not 'the' QA MCP. Configuration belongs to m3-l4.

groundingSources.title: Playwright 1.59 release notes (April 2026)
groundingSources.relevance: Evidence that the classic test stack itself is becoming agent-native (browser.bind across CLI/MCP/test, page.screencast video receipts, npx playwright trace for headless CI agents, @playwright/cli token-efficient alternative to MCP).
claimsSupported: Classic Playwright stack is converging with agent tooling; AI-native and classic are not a binary choice.
Token-efficient @playwright/cli coexists with Playwright MCP; MCP is not the only path.
groundingSources.notes: Deep details belong to m3-l4; m3-l1 only uses this to defend the dual-layer stack recommendation.

groundingSources.title: Claude Code Hooks Guide
groundingSources.relevance: Authoritative source that PostToolUse hooks with Edit|Write matcher exist and are the documented way to trigger tests after the agent edits a file. Lets m3-l1 strategically recommend post-edit hook gates without teaching configuration.
claimsSupported: Post-edit test-trigger hooks are a real, documented mechanism in 2026.
Guidance to keep hooks fast and end with non-blocking exit codes is official.
groundingSources.notes: Lesson should describe the category (post-edit hook) and stay tool-agnostic. Cursor/Codex equivalents are not covered here.

groundingSources.title: ISTQB risk-based testing (glossary + foundation-level material)
groundingSources.relevance: Anchors risk-first prioritization in an established testing discipline (impact x likelihood, highest-risk-first triage) so the lesson does not read like AI hype.
claimsSupported: Risk-based testing is a long-standing discipline, not a 2026 AI invention.
Impact x likelihood prioritization is industry-standard, applicable to solo developers as a triage method.
groundingSources.notes: Use as a single foundational reference; do not turn the lesson into an ISTQB summary.

groundingSources.title: Introducing the Model Context Protocol
groundingSources.relevance: Confirms MCP is now a cross-vendor open standard. Justifies treating 'QA-oriented MCP' as a stable category even if specific servers move fast.
claimsSupported: MCP is supported across major model vendors in 2026, so naming the category is safe even when names of specific servers change.
groundingSources.notes: Lesson does not teach MCP; only anchors the category.

groundingSources.title: Stagehand (browserbase/stagehand) + Browserbase
groundingSources.relevance: Second concrete AI-native browser-control example alongside Playwright MCP. Stagehand v3 dropped Playwright in favor of CDP, exposes act/extract/observe/agent primitives, supports MCP integration. Lets the lesson keep its 'category, not commitment' framing credible.
claimsSupported: More than one production-grade AI-native browser-control stack exists in 2026-05; learner choice depends on project shape.
DOM-driven AI browser automation is currently more reliable than vision-only automation on common tasks.
groundingSources.notes: Mention as alternative; do not benchmark in lesson prose. Configuration is m3-l4 territory.

groundingSources.title: Vitest 4.1 release (March 2026)
groundingSources.relevance: Current state of the recommended unit/integration runner. 'Run Related Tests' command directly supports the post-edit hook story; GitHub Actions reporter feeds the CI gate story.
claimsSupported: Vitest is the current agent-friendly default for unit/integration in JS/TS.
Native 'run related tests' aligns with hook-after-edit workflows without custom scripting.
groundingSources.notes: Configuration belongs to m3-l2; m3-l1 only names it as default.

groundingSources.title: Mock Service Worker (MSW) 2.0
groundingSources.relevance: Current stable line for API mocking across Vitest, Storybook, and the browser. Default network-mock layer in the recommended stack.
claimsSupported: MSW remains the agent-friendly default for API mocking in 2026.
Shared handler definition across test, story, and browser environments is a real, supported pattern.
groundingSources.notes: No risk of obsolescence in 2026; safe to name.

groundingSources.title: axe-core / @axe-core/cli (Deque)
groundingSources.relevance: Current accessibility engine. Same accessibility tree it inspects is the surface AI agents (e.g., Playwright MCP) navigate, so a11y hygiene now also serves agent reliability, not only compliance.
claimsSupported: axe-core is the current default for a11y testing in 2026.
Accessibility tree quality is load-bearing for agent navigation, not only for screen readers.
groundingSources.notes: Useful framing for the lesson: a11y is a quality and agent-reliability concern.

groundingSources.title: Repomix
groundingSources.relevance: Primary recommendation for the Deep Dive 'brief-from-outside' path. Packs a repo into Markdown/XML/JSON for an external LLM, with token counting, security scan, and MCP server integration.
claimsSupported: Repomix is current and well-maintained in 2026; safe primary recommendation for the brief-from-outside path.
LLM-with-large-context is a complementary, not competing, path to in-repo agents.
groundingSources.notes: GitIngest belongs in the same section as a lighter-weight alternative; do not equate the two.

groundingSources.title: AGENTS.md cross-tool agent rules convention (Augment Code + Cursor docs)
groundingSources.relevance: Establishes that persistent rules-files like AGENTS.md and Cursor .mdc rules are a real cross-tool convention in 2026. m3-l1 references this only as background - context/foundation/test-plan.md is positioned as a standalone living document, not as another rules-file layered on AGENTS.md.
claimsSupported: Persistent, scoped rules-files are the current cross-tool standard for guiding code agents.

AGENTS.md / Cursor rules cover project-wide agent behavior; project-specific operational documents (like context/foundation/test-plan.md) can live alongside them without being merged into the rules hierarchy.
groundingSources.notes: Use only as background for the M2 rules-file hierarchy. Do not present context/foundation/test-plan.md as a rules-file in lesson prose.

groundingSources.title: Multimodal UI evaluation (Ionio practitioner post + arXiv 2604.25420 + BentoML VLM guide)
groundingSources.relevance: Triangulates the AI-native multimodal E2E recommendation. Confirms vision models (GPT-4o, Claude 3 Sonnet/Opus, Gemini 2.5) align with expert UI assessment often enough to supplement, not replace, expert review.
claimsSupported: Multimodal LLMs are a useful supplement for UI evaluation in 2026, not a replacement for expert review.
Combining visual cues with text guidance materially improves automated UI test generation accuracy.
groundingSources.notes: Drafter must keep 'when NOT to use' tight: cost per vision call, false positives on layout drift, flaky vision output. Avoid any specific percentage in lesson prose.

groundingSources.title: Anthropic Computer Use + OpenAI CUA — vision-driven fallback
groundingSources.relevance: Names the vision-driven fallback path for UIs the DOM-driven stack cannot reach (canvas-only, image-driven, anti-bot, native desktop). Lets the lesson acknowledge the option without making it the default recommendation.
claimsSupported: Anthropic Computer Use and OpenAI CUA are the current production-leaning options for vision-driven UI automation in 2026.
Vision-driven automation is slower and more expensive per action than DOM-driven approaches and should be reserved for surfaces DOM-driven tools cannot reach.
groundingSources.notes: Frame as fallback, not default. Do not quote per-action costs in lesson prose; magnitude changes faster than the rest of the stack.

groundingSources.title: Visual regression baseline — Argos, Lost Pixel, Percy
groundingSources.relevance: Counterweight to multimodal-UI-evaluation hype. Argos and Lost Pixel deliberately use deterministic pixel/odiff diffing rather than AI; Percy adds AI on top. Lets the lesson position multimodal review as one option among several, not the default.
claimsSupported: Mature deterministic visual-regression tooling is the production default in 2026; multimodal evaluation is additive, not a replacement.
Choice between deterministic and model-judged diff is a 'when NOT to use multimodal' signal worth naming.
groundingSources.notes: Important for keeping the AI-native section honest: not every visual quality problem needs an LLM.

newClaimsIntroduced: `/10x-test-plan` is an orchestration layer over the M2 cycle (/10x-new -> /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement), not a single-prompt guide generator.

`context/foundation/test-plan.md` lives in `context/foundation/` and is jointly a strategy document and the persistent memory of the quality rollout; files on disk, not chat history, are the source of state.
Greenfield gate: if the project is only PRD and notes without code, the skill stops and redirects to `/10x-shape` and `/10x-prd`.
Signal-vs-knowledge boundary is explicit: `context/foundation/test-plan.md` stays at the risk/signal level, `/10x-research` produces file:line knowledge in a dedicated phase.

The skill actively enforces the boundary by reformulating implementation-leaning risks ('brak retry w session.ts') into user-felt scenarios and by replacing file names in the source column with interview-question number, PRD line, or churn directory.

Plan-research is bidirectional: when `/10x-research` discovers that a failure lives elsewhere than the hot-spot suggested, a subsequent `/10x-test-plan` run proposes a correction to the existing phase.
Hot-spot scan scope confirmation is mandatory and uses concrete workspace + exclusion examples (dist, coverage, fixtures, lockfile, snapshots) before the skill scans anything.
The interview is adaptive: skill skips the 'what feels undertested' question on greenfield and asks 'where is the biggest gap that worries you' on sparse-suite projects.
Test-budget exclusions are a first-class section: the last interview question feeds a separate exclusions section in the plan with rationale that survives team rotation.
Cookbook Patterns start as placeholders ('TBD - see §3 Phase 1') and are filled per phase as the last step of each rollout phase.
Operational modes `/10x-test-plan --status` and `/10x-test-plan --refresh` are the documented re-entry paths after a break or staleness.
Lesson explicitly defines a 7-question contract that `context/foundation/test-plan.md` must answer after the lesson.
claimsRemoved: `context/foundation/test-plan.md` is NOT positioned as a domain-specific rules-file wired into AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md as the primary integration mechanism; the draft treats it as a standalone living document in `context/foundation/` that the skill reads on each invocation.
'Fresh session smoke test - agent answers with risk map' as the integration check is replaced by `/10x-test-plan --status` reading state from disk.

Phased Rollout statuses (not started / change opened / researched / planned / implementing / complete) are no longer enumerated in lesson prose; statuses live inside the skill, the lesson only describes phases as a state machine implicitly via --status.
Classic-layer table (unit/integration/contract/e2e/visual/a11y/manual) with cost-x-signal mapping is no longer required in lesson prose.
AI-native table with per-row 'kiedy NIE używać' for post-edit hooks, QA MCP, multimodal e2e, agent-driven exploratory, debug-as-test, and vision fallback is no longer required in lesson prose.
Recommended JS/TS stack default (Vitest + Testing Library + MSW + Playwright + axe-core) is no longer named explicitly in lesson prose.
Quality gates required/recommended/optional table tied to rollout phases is no longer in lesson prose (only mentioned as a section to inspect during the practical task).

Full step-by-step handoff loop `/10x-test-plan -> /10x-new -> /clear -> /10x-research -> /clear -> /10x-plan -> /clear -> /10x-implement` is no longer enumerated in prose; the lesson shows the cycle as a four-step list after the signal-vs-knowledge section.
10xcards / fiszki z AI as a running scenario across the whole lesson is no longer used; the lesson is project-agnostic.
Repomix / GitIngest as a Deep Dive 'brief-from-outside' second-opinion path is no longer required; they remain only in Materiały dodatkowe.
Prework references 3.5 (multimodal model criteria) and 4.2 (test + CI/CD as project minimum) are no longer cited in lesson prose.
Eighth contract question (quality gates) is no longer required; lesson currently states 7 questions.
neighboringLessonReferencesChanged: m2-l5 bridge-out is now the direct bridge-in for m3-l1: faster parallel shipping exposes the quality gap, and `/10x-status` is used to surface it.
M2 cycle (/10x-new, /10x-research, /10x-plan, /10x-implement) is the explicit downstream of `/10x-test-plan` handoffs - no new sub-workflow is invented for quality.
m3-l2 starts from the first rollout phase in `context/foundation/test-plan.md` and consumes `research.md` from the first change folder.
m3-l3 / m3-l4 / m3-l5 implement layers that m3-l1 now names only generally; the detailed cataloguing of AI-native layers has been pushed down into those lessons.
potentialDuplicates: Rules-file hierarchy duplicates m1-l4 if re-taught. Boundary: m1-l4 teaches rules-files; m3-l1 only references them as input and does not position test-plan.md as another rules-file.
CI/CD duplicates m1-l5 if YAML or pipeline setup appears. Boundary: m3-l1 names quality gates as a section the skill produces; m1-l5 owns pipeline setup.
/10x-new, /10x-research, /10x-plan, /10x-implement duplicate m2 if re-taught. Boundary: m3-l1 only positions the cycle as downstream; m2 owns it.
`/10x-impl-review-ci` duplicates m2-l5 if presented as the main testing mechanism. Boundary: m3-l1 positions it as one automated review gate next to tests.
Unit/integration test authoring duplicates m3-l2. Boundary: m3-l1 maps risks to test forms but does not write tests.
MCP and multimodal e2e details duplicate m3-l4. Boundary: m3-l1 does not even name the AI-native layer in detail anymore.
unsupportedFacts: Whether `/10x-test-plan --status` and `--refresh` are the final flag names in the shipped toolkit - draft uses them consistently.
Whether the `context/foundation/` directory convention is course-wide or specific to projects on the /10x cycle.
Whether the default hot-spot scan window is 'last 30 days' (used in earlier schema notes, not stated explicitly in current draft prose) - needs verification against shipped skill behavior.
videoTextMismatches: Video scenario is not yet finalized; the draft embeds a single video at the end of 'Sygnał, nie diagnoza' covering the whole skill usage in one demonstration. Detailed beat-by-beat alignment is the job of the video-scenario artifact.
needsHumanDecision: Whether `/10x-test-plan` and `context/foundation/test-plan.md` are the final canonical names in the shipped toolkit (draft assumes yes; schema follows the draft).
Whether the lesson title 'Plan testów z AI: quality gates, test-plan i priorytety' should be updated, given that 'quality gates' is no longer a major prose section.

Whether m3-l1 should reintroduce any of the removed content (classic-layer table, AI-native table with 'when NOT to use', JS/TS stack default, quality gates tiers, full handoff chain, 10xcards running scenario, Repomix/GitIngest Deep Dive) before RC, or whether the lighter draft is the intended final shape.
Whether the contract-question count should remain 7 in the draft or be brought back to 8 (e.g., by adding a quality-gates question) for symmetry with original spec.
Whether the `context/foundation/` directory convention is course-wide or specific to projects using the /10x cycle.
Whether the exact phase names suggested by the skill (e.g., Critical-path coverage / Integration around hot-spots / AI-native layer / Quality-gates wiring) are prescriptive or illustrative.

lessons.title: Od planu do testów: implementacja unitów z Agentem
lessons.titleEn: From Plan to Tests: Implementing Unit Tests with the Agent
owns: risk-driven test generation przez jeden cykl /10x-new -> /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement: punktem wyjscia jest ryzyko z context/foundation/test-plan.md, nigdy plik ani coverage target

operacjonalizacja problemu wyroczni (oracle problem) wprowadzonego w m3-l1: /10x-research dostarcza opis oczekiwanego zachowania, ktorego nie da sie wyczytac z samej implementacji - to on, nie skladnia, jest sednem testu; m3-l2 nie wprowadza pojecia od nowa, tylko poglebia je przykladem getNextInterval
checklist trzech anti-wzorcow LLM-test: mirror implementacji, happy-path-only, missing edge cases

weryfikacja asercji przez celowe psucie kodu produkcyjnego (reczna, uproszczona forma testow mutacji): poluzuj walidacje / zmien operator / dodaj console.warn z wrazliwymi danymi i sprawdz, czy test spada na czerwono

rytual review jakosci testow we wspolpracy z agentem: /10x-impl-review jako pierwsze sito + bezposrednie pytanie o anti-wzorce, re-prompt na konkretna wade zamiast akceptacji pierwszego outputu (operacjonalizacja trybu korepetytora z preworku 1.3)

Vitest jako agent-friendly default dla unit/integration w JS/TS; vi.spyOn(global, 'fetch') zamiast MSW i srodowisko node zamiast happy-dom jako tanszy domyslny wybor wg reguly cost x signal (MSW / Testing Library / happy-dom jako opcja, nie default; bez glebi konfiguracji)

pozycjonowanie testow mutacji jako 4. warstwy weryfikacji w Deep Dive: Stryker jako canonical JS/TS, mutation score vs. coverage % jako dwie rozne miary, transferable do mutmut/Stryker.NET/PIT/cargo-mutants/mull

/10x-tdd jako opcjonalny tryb test-first (RED -> GREEN -> REFACTOR) dla faz z nowym kodem: ten sam plan.md, ta sama sekcja ## Progress i te same fazy co /10x-implement, zmienia tylko kolejnosc; zmusza agenta do ujawnienia asercji zanim napisze kod; heurystyka 'jesli umiesz nazwac pierwszy czerwony test jednym zdaniem, rozwaz /10x-tdd'; zaklada istniejace srodowisko testowe (nie bootstrapuje); oba tryby mozna mieszac w obrebie jednego planu
referencesOnly: context/foundation/test-plan.md jako strategia + risk map + Phased Rollout (m3-l1) - cykl startuje od top niepokrytego ryzyka albo aktualnej fazy rollout'u
context/changes/<change-id>/research.md jako research anchor (file/function/module) + wyrocznia; sam research jest produktem /10x-research z m2
/10x-new jako krok bootstrapujacy zmiane, otwierajacy cykl
/10x-research jako knowledge-extraction workflow dostarczajacy code anchors + wyrocznie (m2)
/10x-plan jako workflow planowania slice'a, ktory rozbija prace na fazy (m2)
/10x-implement jako krok generujacy realne testy
/10x-test-plan jako skill utrzymujacy kursanta w obrebie aktualnej fazy rollout'u z m3-l1
/10x-impl-review jako pierwsze sito oceny jakosci wygenerowanych zmian
hooki triggerujace testy po edycji (m3-l3)
e2e Playwright + MCP browser automation (m3-l4)
debug-as-test workflow (m3-l5)
prompt jako kontrakt (prework 3.2)
testy utrwalajace bledne zalozenia jako typowy bug LLM (prework 3.1)
tryb korepetytora (prework 1.3)
Vitest jako default + projekt z testami i CI/CD (prework 4.1/4.2)
AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md (m1-l4) jako miejsce na krotka instrukcje o dodatkowej bramce jakosci (np. mutation testing) - nie na prompt-template, ktorego lekcja swiadomie nie formalizuje
problem wyroczni jako pojecie wprowadzone w m3-l1 (przyklad calculateDiscount + cztery linie obrony) - m3-l2 odwoluje sie do niego, nie wprowadza go od nowa
mustNotCover: context/foundation/test-plan.md jako strategia jakosci (m3-l1)
sekcja Testing Strategy w /10x-plan jako osobny nosnik testow dla nowego slice'a - m3-l2 nie uczy tej sciezki (testy ida przez cykl /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement, opcjonalnie /10x-tdd)
/10x-plan jako workflow planowania (m2)
konfiguracja hookow triggerujacych testy po edycji (m3-l3)
kod e2e Playwright, konfiguracja Playwright MCP, multimodalne scenariusze (m3-l4)
debug-as-test workflow w detalu (m3-l5)
code review podczas implementacji slice'a (m2-l3)
Vitest configuration deep (assume z preworku 4.1/4.2)
Stryker konfiguracja deep (Deep Dive: concept + demo + interpretacja, nie full setup)
benchmark Stryker vs konkurenci (tylko named jako transferable kategoria)
coverage instrumentation deep
property-based testing i fuzzing (wzmianka przy okazji mutation, nie uczone)
test design patterns klasyczne (AAA, given-when-then) - assume basic
learningOutcomes: Kursant generuje z agentem unit/integration test dla top niepokrytego ryzyka albo aktualnej fazy rollout'u z context/foundation/test-plan.md, przepuszczajac je przez cykl /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement i uzywajac research anchor + wyroczni z research.md.

Kursant uruchamia /10x-research zanim poprosi o jakikolwiek test i wylawia z niego trzy rzeczy: gdzie ryzyko przechodzi przez kod, jakie zachowanie udowodniloby ochrone (wyrocznia), i jaki jest najtanszy test lapiacy to ryzyko.
Kursant rozpoznaje w outpucie agenta trzy anti-wzorce LLM-test (mirror implementacji, happy-path-only, missing edges) i re-promptuje na konkretna wade zamiast akceptowac pierwszy output.

Kursant ocenia jakosc testow we wspolpracy z agentem (/10x-impl-review + bezposrednie pytanie o anti-wzorce) i weryfikuje asercje przez celowe psucie kodu produkcyjnego - wie, ze zielony test po zepsuciu kodu niczego nie chroni (rytual korepetytora z preworku 1.3).

Kursant interpretuje mutation score na wlasnym module i rozumie, ze coverage % i mutation score to dwie rozne miary jakosci - wybiera swiadomie, kiedy uruchomic Strykera (krytyczne moduly, nie kazdy commit).

Kursant wie, kiedy uzyc opcjonalnego /10x-tdd (faza z nowym kodem, pierwszy czerwony test daje sie nazwac jednym zdaniem) zamiast /10x-implement, rozumie cykl RED -> GREEN -> REFACTOR jako sposob na wymuszenie asercji przed implementacja i moze mieszac oba tryby w obrebie jednego plan.md.
videoPlaceholders: Deep Dive: Stryker na zywo - prowadzacy uruchamia npx stryker run na module zabezpieczonym testem dla ryzyka z test-plan.md, pokazuje raport HTML i mutation score, wskazuje przezytego mutanta z diffem, ponawia prompt o test zabijajacy te mutacje, po czym uruchamia Stryker ponownie i pokazuje wzrost mutation score. (Jedyny placeholder obecny w draycie po backporcie 2026-05-28; wczesniejsze V1-V6 odnosily sie do struktury z Workflow A/B, ktora draft porzucil.)

groundingSources.title: 10x TDD skill
groundingSources.relevance: Confirms /10x-tdd is the explicit plan-to-tests pipeline: it reads a plan, extracts testable scenarios, analyzes the stack, proposes a focused test plan, persists it, and writes behavior-level tests after approval.
claimsSupported: /10x-tdd is the course-native path for turning a /10x-plan artifact into executable tests.
Generated tests should describe observable behavior, avoid implementation details, minimize mocks, and group by behavior rather than function.
groundingSources.notes: Use to refine Workflow B: /10x-plan carries test-shaped intent; /10x-tdd is the first-class test-writing skill.

groundingSources.title: 10x Plan skill
groundingSources.relevance: Current /10x-plan template includes automated/manual success criteria and a Testing Strategy section, but not a named first-class Test Scenarios section.
claimsSupported: /10x-plan can carry test-shaped success criteria and Testing Strategy content.
m3-l2 should not overclaim that /10x-plan itself writes tests or already owns a named Test Scenarios artifact.
groundingSources.notes: If lesson prose uses 'Test Scenarios', frame it as a recommended subsection or handoff to /10x-tdd.

groundingSources.title: 10x Test Plan skill
groundingSources.relevance: Confirms context/foundation/test-plan.md is a durable strategy + Phased Rollout document consumed before generating tests, planning slices, or wiring quality gates, and that /10x-tdd is downstream.
claimsSupported: Ścieżka A should start from the highest untreated risk or the current rollout phase in context/foundation/test-plan.md, not from a file list.
Generating tests without consulting the risk map is a defect in this course workflow.
groundingSources.notes: m3-l2 consumes context/foundation/test-plan.md; m3-l1 owns producing it. Verify the SKILL.md path is still 10x-test-plan in the shipped toolkit before draft freeze.

groundingSources.title: Vitest 4.1 release notes
groundingSources.relevance: Current evidence that Vitest is adapting to AI coding-agent workflows through Run Related Tests, GitHub Actions summaries, and an agent reporter with reduced output.
claimsSupported: Vitest remains a current agent-friendly JS/TS unit/integration runner.
Run-related-tests and compact agent output fit m3-l2 manual reruns and m3-l3 hook automation.
groundingSources.notes: Keep configuration details out of m3-l2; use as stack freshness evidence.

groundingSources.title: Testing Library guiding principles
groundingSources.relevance: Stable source for behavior-level component testing and avoiding component-instance or implementation-detail assertions.
claimsSupported: Tests should exercise components the way users use them.
m3-l2's anti-mirror checklist should prefer observable behavior over implementation shape.
groundingSources.notes: Old but intentionally stable guidance; useful in Materialy dodatkowe.

groundingSources.title: Mock Service Worker and Vitest request mocking guide
groundingSources.relevance: MSW provides client-agnostic network mocks reused across tools; Vitest recommends it for HTTP, GraphQL, and WebSocket request mocking.
claimsSupported: MSW is the recommended network-boundary mock for Vitest integration tests.
Integration tests should mock network boundaries without mocking the domain behavior under test.
groundingSources.notes: Secondary URL checked: https://vitest.dev/guide/mocking/requests.html

groundingSources.title: Stryker Mutator docs
groundingSources.relevance: Authoritative grounding for mutation testing, mutation score, StrykerJS support for JS/TS frameworks, and incremental/scoped mutation runs.
claimsSupported: Mutation testing checks whether tests fail when production code is deliberately changed.
Coverage and mutation score answer different questions.
Stryker is a canonical JS/TS mutation testing example but not a full-course setup topic.
groundingSources.notes: Use in Deep Dive only; avoid full setup tutorial.

groundingSources.title: Large Language Models for Unit Testing: A Systematic Literature Review
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Quanjun Zhang, Chunrong Fang, Siqi Gu, Ye Shang, Zhenyu Chen, Liang Xiao
groundingSources.relevance: Research overview showing LLM unit-test generation is active but still has unresolved challenges and needs hybrid approaches.
claimsSupported: LLM-generated tests can help, but one-shot generation is not enough as a quality strategy.
Human review and feedback loops remain part of responsible AI-assisted unit testing.
groundingSources.notes: Use for research posture, not practical tool instructions.

groundingSources.title: TestForge: Feedback-Driven, Agentic Test Suite Generation
groundingSources.relevance: Supports iterative test generation with feedback from executions and coverage reports, and treats mutation score as a separate quality signal.
claimsSupported: Agentic test generation should use feedback loops rather than a single prompt.
Mutation score is a useful additional signal for evaluating generated tests.
groundingSources.notes: Avoid importing benchmark numbers into lesson prose unless explicitly needed.

groundingSources.title: Test vs Mutant: Adversarial LLM Agents for Robust Unit Test Generation
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Pengyu Chang, Yixiong Fang, Silin Chen, Yuling Shi, Beijun Shen, Xiaodong Gu
groundingSources.relevance: Current research signal that mutation-guided adversarial loops are relevant to robust LLM-generated tests and corner-case discovery.
claimsSupported: Mutation testing is relevant to current agentic test-generation research, not only a legacy testing technique.
Coverage and mutation scores can be combined to improve bug-detection robustness.
groundingSources.notes: arXiv preprint; use as optional Deep Dive material, not an industry-standard proof.

groundingSources.title: Benchmarking LLMs for Unit Test Generation from Real-World Functions (ULT / UnLeakedTestbench)
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Dong Huang, Jie M. Zhang (King's College London), Mark Harman (Meta/UCL), Qianru Zhang, Mingzhe Du, See-Kiong Ng (NUS); accepted to ACM TOSEM (DOI 10.1145/3805043)
groundingSources.relevance: Peer-reviewed evidence that prior LLM test-generation benchmarks overstate ability due to data contamination and structurally trivial functions; on a clean benchmark of real-world functions, accuracy and coverage roughly halve.
claimsSupported: Impressive LLM test-generation results on simple/toy code do not generalize to real-world codebases; quality drops sharply as function complexity rises.
Apparent LLM test-writing skill is partly memorization, not reasoning, which is why human review and feedback loops are required.
groundingSources.notes: Strongest source for the lesson's belief-busters ('agent can test any file', 'green means good'). Cite the real-world-vs-toy gap as framing; do not quote the Python-specific percentages in learner prose.

groundingSources.title: LLMs for Unit Test Generation: Achievements, Challenges, and Opportunities
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Bei Chu, Yang Feng, Kui Liu, Zhaoqiang Guo, Yichi Zhang, Hange Shi, Zifan Nan, Baowen Xu (Nanjing University, State Key Lab for Novel Software Technology)
groundingSources.relevance: Survey synthesizing many studies: the oracle problem (deciding what to assert) is the core limitation, coverage does not correlate with fault detection, and reliable results come from LLMs coupled with deterministic tools and iterative repair, not one-shot generation.
claimsSupported: Coverage is not assertion strength; mutation score is the more honest signal (supports the Deep Dive framing).
The prompt -> output -> review/run -> re-prompt loop is the responsible pattern, not single-shot test generation.
groundingSources.notes: Unreviewed arXiv preprint (v2, 2025-12-30) from a reputable testing group. Good for framing claims; do not quote a single benchmark number as settled.

groundingSources.title: Quality Assessment of Python Tests Generated by LLMs
groundingSources.relevance: Empirical study showing incorrect assertions are the most common defect in LLM-generated tests, reinforcing that the assertion (oracle) is the weakest point to verify separately.
claimsSupported: Faulty assertions are the most frequent error class in LLM-generated tests, which is why m3-l2 requires verifying the assertion by deliberately breaking the code.
Green tests are not evidence of protection; assertion quality must be checked independently.
groundingSources.notes: Cited in Materialy dodatkowe of the m3-l2 draft. Use for the 'verify the assertion' framing, not benchmark numbers.

groundingSources.title: On the Flakiness of LLM-Generated Tests for DBMS
groundingSources.relevance: Study on LLM-generated tests transferring flaky assertions from context and being dominated by unordered-result errors, supporting the edge-case and assertion-verification posture of the lesson.
claimsSupported: LLM-generated tests can import unstable assertions from context, so edge cases and assertion verification matter.
Apparent test correctness can hide order-dependent or environment-dependent flakiness.
groundingSources.notes: arXiv preprint cited in Materialy dodatkowe of the m3-l2 draft. Optional supporting signal, not an industry-standard proof.

newClaimsIntroduced: Backport 2026-05-28: lesson stands on a single cycle /10x-new -> /10x-research -> /10x-plan -> /10x-implement, with /10x-test-plan (keeps learner inside the rollout phase) and /10x-impl-review (first quality sieve) as supporting skills.
Stack default is Vitest + vi.spyOn(global, 'fetch') + node environment; MSW / Testing Library / happy-dom demoted to options per the cost x signal rule.

Case study 10xcards runs Phase 1 with two risks (#1 broken OpenRouter response, #7 pasted-text leak to DB/logs) across 5 sub-phases (1.1 Bootstrap -> 1.5 Cookbook+sync), where /10x-research changes the goal from bug-fix to regression guard.
Three LLM-test anti-patterns (mirror implementacji, happy-path-only, missing edge cases) are the explicit pre-commit checklist for m3-l2, distinct from the broader E2E anti-pattern set in m3-l4.

Assertion verification by deliberately breaking production code (manual, simplified mutation testing) is the in-lesson quality gate before commit; a test that stays green after the code is broken protects nothing.
Stryker / mutation testing is positioned only as a 4th-layer Deep Dive verification, with coverage % and mutation score framed as two different questions (not better/worse).

Metrics-in-prose: the draft quotes only the verified ULT toy-vs-real coverage gap (~92%->~45% lines, ~82%->~30% branches) in the opener; the broader figure set from the prior override is NOT present in the current draft.
claimsRemoved: Workflow A/B dual-framing and a dedicated Testing Strategy section in /10x-plan as a separate test carrier — still out of scope; the draft teaches one cycle. NOTE: /10x-tdd itself is back in owns/learningOutcomes (reinstated 2026-05-30) as an optional test-first mode the draft still teaches.
prompt-template as a standalone owned concept — the draft drives test generation through the skill cycle (risk + research + oracle), not a formalized template artifact.
Deep Dive line 'Nie potrzebujesz importować z tych badań metryk' — removed; superseded by the deliberate metrics-in-prose decision.

Prior author override (2026-05-28) claiming compile-failure rates, ~75% missing-input, 64% assertion-error share, GPT-4-vs-EvoSuite coverage and 63% unordered-collection flakiness in learner prose — these figures are not in the current draft.
neighboringLessonReferencesChanged: The single workflow explicitly consumes context/foundation/test-plan.md AND context/changes/<change-id>/research.md from m3-l1 + m2, not just the risk map.
Test authoring routes through /10x-implement by default (with /10x-test-plan and /10x-impl-review as supporting skills); /10x-tdd is the optional test-first mode for phases that add new code.
potentialDuplicates: Anti-pattern review ritual vs m3-l4 E2E anti-pattern checklist - boundary clear: m3-l2 owns 3 unit-level anti-patterns; m3-l4 extends with E2E-specific ones (flaky CSS, state coupling, waitForTimeout, missing cleanup).
Stack mention (Vitest + Testing Library + MSW) duplicates m3-l1 grounding - boundary clear: m3-l1 names them as default, m3-l2 uses them in the loop but does not teach configuration.
unsupportedFacts: Which concrete 10xcards module will be stable and small enough for the Stryker mutation demo without turning the Deep Dive into setup work.
Whether the course repo will ship a ready Stryker config for the demo or only show mutation-report interpretation conceptually.
ULT (2508.00408) accuracy/Pass@5 figures are NOT cited because two source reads disagreed (41.32%/91.79% vs 12.57%/51.93%); only coverage and mutation figures, consistent across reads, are used.

All cited research uses Java/Python and mostly older/smaller models, not the frontier agents learners use in 2026; the draft states this scope caveat and frames the metrics as pattern evidence, not project-specific predictions.
needsHumanDecision: Resolved 2026-05-30: /10x-tdd reinstated in the contract (owns + learningOutcomes) because the draft is the source of truth and still teaches it as an optional test-first mode; the 2026-05-28 removal is superseded. Only the Workflow A/B dual-framing and a dedicated Testing Strategy section in /10x-plan remain out of scope.
Decide whether m3-l2 Deep Dive uses a prepared Stryker config in the demo repo. Recommendation from grounding: yes, if the video needs a live run; otherwise keep setup out of prose.
Decide whether metrics-in-prose stays limited to the single ULT toy-vs-real coverage gap cited in the current draft, or expands to the broader figure set from the prior override.

lessons.title: Hooki i triggery: Agent, który sam reaguje na błędy
lessons.titleEn: Hooks and triggers: an agent that reacts to errors on its own
owns: Three-layer local quality pipeline: per-edit agent hooks → pre-commit git hooks → pre-push checks as complementary gates with distinct trigger cadences
PostToolUse hook configuration for lint, typecheck, and scoped test triggers — practical wiring, not strategic framing (that's m3-l1)
Scope-aware check matching: per-file matching via tool_input.file_path, glob-based filters, and practical patterns for targeting checks to change scope
Hook lifecycle as transferable mental model: trigger → match → check → signal, with Claude Code as primary deep example and cross-tool transfer table
Context injection as the feedback mechanism: hook stdout → agent sees lint/type errors → agent self-corrects trivial issues in its existing edit loop
Git pre-commit layer as complementary commit-time gate (staged-file scoping, lint + typecheck + test on commit). Lefthook recommended as agent-friendly option, concept is tool-agnostic.
context/foundation/test-plan.md quality gates as the source of what checks to wire — hooks operationalize gates declared in m3-l1
Hook performance awareness: fast hooks for per-edit (lint/format), heavier checks at commit/push time, and the cost of slow hooks blocking the agent loop
referencesOnly: context/foundation/test-plan.md as strategy + risk source (m3-l1)
AI-native testing patterns at strategic level including post-edit hooks (m3-l1 — strategic framing only)
Unit test authoring with agent (m3-l2 — hooks trigger tests that m3-l2 taught to write)
/10x-plan workflow and implementation cycle (m2)
E2E Playwright + MCP browser automation (m3-l4)
Debug-as-test workflow and stack trace diagnosis (m3-l5)
Rules-file hierarchy: AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md (m1-l4)
Parallel agent sessions and worktrees (m2-l5)
Prework safety discipline: clean repo, tests, review, diffs (prework 2.4)
Prework tutor mode as model for hook feedback (prework 1.3)
mustNotCover: context/foundation/test-plan.md creation or risk prioritization (m3-l1)
Unit/integration test authoring, Vitest config, Testing Library, MSW (m3-l2)
E2E tests, Playwright config, MCP browser automation, multimodal scenarios (m3-l4)
Debug strategy from stack trace to fix, complex failure diagnosis (m3-l5)
Code review methodology and triage (m2-l3)
CI/CD pipeline creation, GitHub Actions YAML, deployment config (m1-l5, m2-l5)
AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md authoring from scratch (m1-l4)
Husky deep configuration or migration (reference Lefthook as recommended path)
Cursor/Windsurf/Codex full setup tutorial (map transfer pattern, don't teach each tool's setup)
learningOutcomes: Kursant konfiguruje PostToolUse hooks (Claude Code) dla auto-lint i typecheck po edycji pliku i weryfikuje, że agent otrzymuje feedback i na niego reaguje.
Kursant konfiguruje scoped test trigger uruchamiający tylko testy powiązane z edytowanym plikiem, korzystając z risk areas z context/foundation/test-plan.md.
Kursant wyjaśnia trzy-warstwowy model lokalnej jakości (per-edit agent hooks → pre-commit git hooks → pre-push) i wskazuje, który check należy do której warstwy.
Kursant ustawia bramkę pre-commit (Lefthook lub istniejący Husky+lint-staged) ze scopingiem na staged files dla lint, typecheck i testów.
Kursant tłumaczy konfigurację hooka z Claude Code na przynajmniej jedno inne narzędzie (Cursor, Codex lub Copilot), demonstrując przenikalność wzorca.
videoPlaceholders: V1 — Otwarcie: kontrast m3-l2 (ręczne testy) vs. PostToolUse hook łapiący błąd natychmiast.
V2 — Konfiguracja pierwszego hooka: PostToolUse lint hook w Claude Code na 10xcards. JSON config → agent edit → hook fires → feedback.
V3 — Scoped test trigger: hook testowy dla high-risk files z context/foundation/test-plan.md. Edycja risk-area pliku (testy) vs. utility file (skip).
V4 — Context injection: agent widzi feedback z hooka i naprawia trywialny błąd w następnej iteracji. Granica: autokorekta, nie debugging.
V5 — Trzy warstwy w praktyce: pełny pipeline na 10xcards z PostToolUse hooks, pre-commit gate i kompletną lokalną pętlą jakości.
V6 — Bridge do m3-l4: lokalne hooki łapią błędy kodu, ale nie regresję wizualną, UX i dostępność. Następna lekcja: przeglądarka i E2E.

unsupportedFacts: Claude Code agent handler type stability status (currently experimental, approved for Deep Dive mention).
Stop hooks in Skills/Plugins contexts have open GitHub issues (#19225, #29767) — PostToolUse hooks not affected.

groundingSources.title: Claude Code Hooks Documentation
groundingSources.relevance: Primary reference for Claude Code hooks: 29 lifecycle events, 5 handler types, exit code semantics, PostToolUse context injection via additionalContext, matcher syntax, config levels.
claimsSupported: Claude Code has ~30 hook events across session, turn, and tool-call cadences.
Five handler types: command, http, mcp_tool, prompt, agent.
PostToolUse matched on Write|Edit receives tool_input.file_path and can inject additionalContext (10,000 char cap).
Exit code 2 = blocking error that signals the agent to fix the issue.
PreToolUse hooks can modify tool input via updatedInput.
groundingSources.notes: Agent handler type is experimental. PostToolUse cannot undo a tool execution. Default timeout: 600s for command/http/mcp_tool, 30s for prompt, 60s for agent.

groundingSources.title: Cursor Hooks Documentation
groundingSources.relevance: Cross-tool comparison: ~18 events including afterFileEdit with edit diffs, failClosed safety option, additional_context injection.
claimsSupported: Cursor supports ~18 hook events with command and prompt handler types.
afterFileEdit receives file_path and edits array — more specific than PostToolUse matcher.
failClosed: true is a unique safety feature (default is fail-open).
groundingSources.notes: Available since Cursor 1.7 (October 2025). No HTTP hook type. Tab hooks are separate from agent hooks.

groundingSources.title: Codex Hooks Documentation
groundingSources.relevance: Cross-tool comparison: 10 events, command-only functional handlers, hash-based trust model.
claimsSupported: Codex has 10 hook events; only command handler type is functional.
PreToolUse interception is incomplete for some tool types.
Hash-based trust model requires explicit user review for project hooks.
groundingSources.notes: GA since early 2026 but less mature than Claude Code. prompt and agent types parsed but skipped.

groundingSources.title: Windsurf Cascade Hooks Documentation
groundingSources.relevance: Cross-tool comparison: 12 events, shell-only, critical limitation — no context injection.
claimsSupported: Windsurf has 12 hook events using snake_case naming.
Windsurf hooks cannot inject feedback to the model (no additionalContext equivalent).
Pre-hooks can block (exit 2) but cannot modify tool input.
groundingSources.notes: The inability to send hook output back to the model is the key differentiator. Target sub-100ms execution per docs.

groundingSources.title: GitHub Copilot Hooks Documentation
groundingSources.relevance: Cross-tool comparison: ~13 events across 3 runtimes, VS Code reads .claude/settings.json as hook source.
claimsSupported: Copilot supports ~13 hook events across cloud agent, VS Code, and CLI.
VS Code Copilot reads .claude/settings.json for hook config — cross-tool compatibility.
Public preview as of March 2026.
groundingSources.notes: Cloud agent is most limited (ephemeral sandbox, ask treated as deny). Two payload formats (camelCase/PascalCase) add complexity.

groundingSources.title: Vitest CLI — related subcommand and --changed flag
groundingSources.relevance: Grounds the scoped test trigger hook: vitest related <path> --run for per-edit hooks, vitest --changed for pre-commit hooks.
claimsSupported: vitest related <path> --run runs only tests that import the specified source file (subcommand, not --related flag).
vitest --changed runs tests related to git-changed files.
Related-test detection uses Vite's static import graph.
groundingSources.notes: Correct syntax is vitest related (subcommand), not vitest --related (flag). Dynamic imports not traced. forceRerunTriggers controls full-suite fallback.

groundingSources.title: Vitest 4.1 release notes — agent reporter
groundingSources.relevance: Agent reporter auto-activates inside AI coding agents, outputs only failures, reduces token usage. Relevant for hook output optimization.
claimsSupported: Vitest 4.1 agent reporter auto-detects AI agent sessions via std-env.
Can be forced with AI_AGENT=1 environment variable.
Run Related Tests is a VS Code extension command, not a new CLI flag.
groundingSources.notes: Useful for hook performance: recommend AI_AGENT=1 in hook environment for minimal output.

groundingSources.title: Lefthook repository and documentation
groundingSources.relevance: Grounds the pre-commit layer recommendation: single lefthook.yml, opt-in parallel execution, {staged_files} interpolation, Go binary.
claimsSupported: Single lefthook.yml config for all git hook types.
Parallel execution via parallel: true (opt-in, not default).
{staged_files} interpolation for scoped checks on staged files.
Go binary with no Node.js dependency. Current version: v2.1.8.
groundingSources.notes: parallel: true is opt-in, not default. The hooks-research.md incorrectly claimed 'parallel by default'. The draft must use explicit parallel: true in examples.

groundingSources.title: 1Password agent-hooks
groundingSources.relevance: Strongest concrete proof of hook architecture convergence: single install script copies the same hook definitions into .cursor/hooks.json, .claude/settings.json, and .windsurf/hooks.json.
claimsSupported: Cross-tool hook convergence is real enough for a single codebase to target multiple tools.
The hook lifecycle pattern (trigger, match, check, signal) transfers across tools.
groundingSources.notes: Enterprise context (1Password). Useful in Materialy dodatkowe and as convergence evidence in the cross-tool transfer beat.

groundingSources.title: Steve Kinney — Self-Testing AI Agents / Git Hooks with Lefthook
groundingSources.relevance: Practitioner endorsement of Lefthook for AI agent workflows. Practical config examples for TypeScript projects.
claimsSupported: Lefthook is recommended by practitioners in the AI coding space for pre-commit quality gates.
Layered model (agent hooks + git hooks + CI) is an emerging community pattern.
groundingSources.notes: Part of a larger course on self-testing AI agents. Useful for Materialy dodatkowe.

groundingSources.title: Speakeasy — AI agent hooks: the interface for governing AI agents
groundingSources.relevance: Frames hooks as the emerging standard interface between humans and agents. Useful framing language for the lesson.
claimsSupported: Hooks are positioning as the standard control interface for AI coding agents.
The pattern is converging across tools toward a shared lifecycle model.
groundingSources.notes: Useful for framing language ('deterministic control layer'). Not a primary source for technical facts.

lessons.title: Testy E2E: Playwright, MCP i multimodalne scenariusze
lessons.titleEn: E2E tests: Playwright, MCP, and multimodal scenarios
owns: Playwright CLI (@playwright/cli) as primary agent-browser interface: setup, snapshot navigation, element refs, session management, token-efficiency rationale vs MCP
Playwright MCP as richer but more expensive alternative: snapshot vs vision mode, --caps system, decision heuristic

Playwright Test Agents (planner/generator/healer) as one PLAN strategy inside /10x-e2e's loop: init, seed test, planner→generator→healer cycle; the alternative PLAN strategy is the skill's prompt-template (single test, no agent init)

/10x-e2e as the risk-driven E2E plan-execution skill — the E2E sibling of /10x-implement and /10x-tdd: reads the same context/changes/<change-id>/plan.md, mutates the same canonical ## Progress, runs the same per-phase commit ritual, and swaps the inner loop for browser-level PLAN→GENERATE→REVIEW→VERIFY gated by an eligibility check (browser-level risk + feature already built + no passing test yet) with deliberate-break verification; planner→generator and the prompt-template are its two PLAN strategies. Mirrors the explanation pattern m3-l2 uses for /10x-tdd.
Accessibility-tree-based browser interaction as default E2E paradigm for agents: snapshot YAML, element refs, role-based targeting

Seed test (seed.spec.ts) as primary E2E quality lever — templates Generator output with: getByRole locators, test independence, state-based waits, risk-tied assertions, data cleanup. The E2E-level counterpart to m3-l2's discipline of risk-tied input + a separately verified behavioral assertion. The canonical seed pattern is captured in the /10x-e2e skill reference (single source).

E2E testing rules and anti-patterns delivered via the /10x-e2e skill as the single source (locator hierarchy getByRole > getByTestId > never CSS, test independence, no waitForTimeout, business-outcome assertions, cleanup, storageState); the toolkit ships a thin CLAUDE.md/.cursor/rules/ pointer carrying 2-3 hard rules + the skill reference, not a full inline rules block.

E2E-specific agent anti-patterns beyond hallucinated assertions: flaky CSS selectors, state coupling between tests, hardcoded waits, missing test data cleanup. Extension of m3-l2's unit-level checklist to E2E.
Test data isolation for E2E — unique identifiers or cleanup-per-test for stable parallel runs. Teardown project + Supabase RLS in Deep Dive.

Vision-based multimodal verification as a light supplement: Playwright vision mode (--caps=vision), screenshot + VLM evaluation for visual-only risks the accessibility tree can't express — DOM-based assertions and deterministic pixel tools (toMatchSnapshot/Argos/Lost Pixel) first, vision only when both fall short; VLM model selection, categories, and per-screenshot cost belong to m3-l5 (vision-as-diagnostic), not here
Risk-driven E2E scenario selection: mapping context/foundation/test-plan.md risk areas to E2E scenarios
storageState for authenticated E2E: pre-authenticated state injection to avoid login loops
referencesOnly: context/foundation/test-plan.md as strategy + risk source (m3-l1)
AI-native testing patterns at strategic level (m3-l1 — strategic framing only)
Unit/integration test authoring, risk-tied input + behavioral-assertion discipline, 3 unit anti-patterns (m3-l2 — E2E builds on that discipline)
PostToolUse hooks and three-layer quality pipeline (m3-l3 — E2E extends pipeline)
Debug-as-test workflow and failure diagnosis (m3-l5)
Vision-based visual diagnosis and VLM model selection (frontier/budget/open-weight) + per-screenshot cost (m3-l5 — vision as a debugging signal there, not a testing topic here)
Rules-file hierarchy: AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md (m1-l4)
CI/CD pipeline as E2E execution environment (m1-l5, m2-l5)
Prompt as contract (prework 3.2)
Token budgets and context management (prework 3.1, 3.3)
Recommended stack includes Playwright (prework 4.1)
Stagehand as alternative AI-native browser automation tool (Deep Dive mention)
OFE/19 best practices (selector hierarchy, test independence) — operationalized as agent output constraints
mustNotCover: context/foundation/test-plan.md creation or risk prioritization (m3-l1)
Unit/integration test authoring, Vitest config, Testing Library, MSW (m3-l2)
Hook configuration, PostToolUse wiring, three-layer pipeline setup (m3-l3)
Debug strategy from stack trace to fix, complex failure diagnosis (m3-l5)
Code review methodology and triage (m2-l3)

AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md authoring from scratch (m1-l4)

Visual regression tool comparison (Argos, Lost Pixel, Percy) beyond brief mention

learningOutcomes: Kursant konfiguruje Playwright CLI i nawiguje po swojej aplikacji przez snapshoty drzewa dostępności, używając element refs zamiast ręcznych selektorów.

Kursant pisze seed test (seed.spec.ts) demonstrujący wzorce jakości: getByRole jako domyślny lokator, niezależność testów, czekanie na stan zamiast na czas, asercja powiązana z ryzykiem, cleanup danych testowych.

Kursant dodaje reguły E2E do pliku reguł projektu (CLAUDE.md / .cursor/rules/) ograniczające output agenta: hierarchia lokatorów, niezależność testów, brak waitForTimeout, asercje na wynik biznesowy, cleanup, storageState.

Kursant uruchamia /10x-e2e na 2-3 najwyższych ryzykach z context/foundation/test-plan.md, prowadząc fazy planu pętlą PLAN→GENERATE→REVIEW→VERIFY (PLAN przez planner→generator albo szablon promptu), przegląda wygenerowane testy pod kątem checklisty antywzorców E2E i weryfikuje każdy test celowym psuciem zachowania, które ma chronić.
Kursant identyfikuje i naprawia anti-wzorce agenta specyficzne dla E2E: hallucynowane asercje, kruche selektory CSS, coupling stanu między testami, hardcoded waits (waitForTimeout), brak cleanup.
Kursant konfiguruje storageState dla uwierzytelnionych testów E2E i ustawia podstawową izolację danych testowych (unikalne identyfikatory lub cleanup per test).
Kursant konfiguruje tryb wizyjny (--caps=vision) i używa screenshot + VLM do weryfikacji elementu UI niedostatecznie opisanego przez drzewo dostępności, wskazując ograniczenia tego podejścia.
Kursant wyjaśnia tradeoff CLI vs MCP (~4x różnica tokenów) i decyduje, kiedy użyć którego w swoim projekcie.
videoPlaceholders: V1 — Hook gap: hook łapiący błąd typów vs regresja wizualna, której żaden hook nie widzi

V4 — Vision supplement: test DOM przechodzi, regresja wizualna wymyka się, vision mode łapie problem
