Back-prop: `ast-grep` is the deterministic structural-confirmation layer (agent=meaning/behavior vs CLI=count/position; AST vs text; `Save` vs `SaveMultiple`; install cross-platform; `sg` alias collides with `setgroups`), with the rule 'count with ast-grep, confirm every zero with grep'; rewrite mode (`-r`, `--interactive`, `-U`, YAML `fix`, `ast-grep scan`) is previewed as the M4-L4 bridge and explicitly NOT used in L3.
neighboringLessonReferencesChanged: M4-L2 handoff strengthened: repo map must produce artifacts consumable by M4-L3, not only a readable architecture diagram.
M4-L4 boundary strengthened: L3 produces evidence and risk read; refactor opportunities remain in M4-L4. L3 previews `ast-grep` rewrite mode as the same patterns that will carry the M4-L4 change.
potentialDuplicates: M4-L2 map construction vs M4-L3 map consumption — draft must avoid rebuilding the map in L3.
React/Mattermost examples in L3 should be used as map-shape calibration only; the main demo should still choose one flow and not become a second Wide Scan lesson.
unsupportedFacts: Connascence primary attribution to Meilir Page-Jones (book titles + dates ~1995/1996, UML 1999) — concept attested, primary citation NOT adversarially verified
Mark Weiser 'Program Slicing' (ICSE 1981 vs IEEE TSE 1984) for blast-radius / change-impact — NOT verified this pass; use idea without hard citation or drop

Current 2025-26 AI-assisted data-flow / sequence-diagram reconstruction and churn/coupling operationalization — emerging, time-sensitive, NOT adversarially verified; frame as practice and re-check near publication

`ast-grep` tool-behavior claims (single Rust binary + prebuilt macOS/Linux/Windows releases, install channels, `sg` alias colliding with `setgroups` on Linux, `-r`/`--interactive`/`-U` semantics, YAML `fix` + `ast-grep scan`) — re-verify against current ast-grep docs before RC and add an ast-grep groundingSource via lesson-grounding.
needsHumanDecision: RESOLVED: ③ report element is named Technical debt (not 'Issues & hot spots'); L3 owns both ② and ③.
Present complexity proxy strictly as indentation-based (Tornhill/CodeScene) or as proxy-agnostic (cyclomatic/cognitive/LOC all viable)? Recommend proxy-agnostic.
Decide whether future revisions should keep Mattermost as the main continuous demo from M4-L2 or switch the learner-facing example to advanced 10xCards for course-project continuity.

lessons.title: Refaktoryzacja z Agentem: testy, zmiany, weryfikacja
lessons.titleEn: Refactoring with the Agent: Tests, Changes, Verification
owns: archetypy aplikacji enterprise jako docelowe kształty modułu: Transaction Script → Table Module → Domain Model z Service Layer (M. Fowler, PoEAA); rozróżnienie złożoności przypadkowej vs istotnej (F. Brooks)
inkrementalne, odwracalne strategie migracji: Strangler Fig oraz Branch by Abstraction zamiast big-bang rewrite (M. Fowler / P. Hammant)
Mikado Method jako metodyczny graf prerekwizytów dużej refaktoryzacji, w parze ze szwami i testami charakteryzującymi legacy (O. Ellnestam & D. Brolund; M. Feathers, WELC)
refactor opportunities jako element raportu ④: rankingowane, uzasadnione kandydatury (current → target archetype) ugruntowane w ADR-ach, historii i non-goals
videoPlaceholders: report-to-decision — jedno nagranie (artifact walkthrough): audyt enumeracji P1–P7, dowody przy werdyktach i odwrócony faworyt, lektura śladów weryfikacji z promptu m4l4-3 (korekty inline, tabela twierdzeń), głośny reranking C1 vs C4, spacer po plan.md (guard-first, czego NIE robimy, fazy) i handoff /10x-implement; scenariusz: lessons/m4-l4/videos/video-report-to-decision.md

groundingSources.title: Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture — pattern catalog (Transaction Script, Table Module, Domain Model, Service Layer)
groundingSources.relevance: Canonical target-shape vocabulary for owns[0]: the domain-logic spectrum the report maps a module against (current → target archetype).
claimsSupported: Transaction Script organizes business logic by procedures, one per presentation request; its strength is simplicity for small-logic apps.
Table Module is a single instance handling business logic for all rows of a table/view.
Domain Model is an object model of the domain incorporating both behavior and data.
Service Layer defines the application boundary and coordinates operations over the domain logic.
groundingSources.notes: Verbatim definitions from Fowler's own catalog (companion to the 2002 PoEAA book), verified 3-0. The trade-off framing anchors right-sized targets (anti-over-engineering).

groundingSources.title: No Silver Bullet — Essence and Accident in Software Engineering
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Frederick P. Brooks, Jr. (IFIP 1986; repr. The Mythical Man-Month Anniversary ed., 1995)
groundingSources.relevance: owns[0] lens: separate accidental cruft from essential (inherent) complexity before proposing a refactor.
claimsSupported: Brooks divides software difficulties into essence (inherent) and accidents (not inherent to the nature of software).
No single development promises an order-of-magnitude improvement in productivity/reliability/simplicity within a decade.
groundingSources.notes: Original wording is 'essence'/'accidents'; 'essential/accidental complexity' is the standard popularized restatement — flag if used.

groundingSources.title: Strangler Fig Application
groundingSources.relevance: owns[1]: the headline incremental, lower-risk alternative to a big-bang rewrite; strongest verified anti-big-bang anchor.
claimsSupported: Incrementally build the new system around the edges of the legacy code, moving behavior over until the old system is supplanted.
The most important reason to prefer a strangler fig over a cut-over rewrite is reduced risk.
groundingSources.notes: Posted 29 June 2004 as 'Strangler Application'; renamed 'Strangler Fig Application' April 2019.

groundingSources.title: Branch By Abstraction
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Martin Fowler (popularized) / Paul Hammant (named the term, credits Stacy Curl)
groundingSources.relevance: owns[1]: release-while-refactoring companion to Strangler Fig — large change behind a stable abstraction while trunk stays shippable.
claimsSupported: A technique for making a large-scale change gradually while still releasing the system regularly.
Introduce an abstraction over the interaction, migrate clients, build the new implementation behind the same interface, swap over, then remove the old one.
groundingSources.notes: Hammant's origin post is dated 2007 (not 2004 — that is Strangler Fig). Attribute the term to Hammant; the practice predates him.

groundingSources.title: The Mikado Method
groundingSources.relevance: owns[2]: the method that turns a daunting refactor into an ordered, verifiable plan; the Mikado Graph is the report's prerequisite-sketch artifact.
claimsSupported: Carefully extract each intertwined dependency until the central goal (the Mikado) is exposed without collapsing the project.
The process yields a Mikado Graph — a dependency roadmap of the refactoring.
groundingSources.notes: Authors/year/graph verified 3-0. The 'revert / resolve-leaves-first' loop is confirmed in substance, not as a verbatim quote.

groundingSources.title: Working Effectively with Legacy Code (sample chapter)
groundingSources.relevance: owns[2]: the safety net under every refactor — seams + characterization tests before touching legacy; sprout/wrap to add behavior safely.
claimsSupported: A seam is a place to alter behavior without editing in that place — the leverage point for getting legacy under test.
A characterization test pins down current behavior so a refactor cannot change it silently.
Sprout/wrap add new behavior in a fresh, tested unit instead of editing untested legacy in place.
groundingSources.notes: Primary source fetched but definitions were not in the adversarially-verified top-25 — re-confirm wording against the sample PDF before learner-facing prose. sourceType is the closest schema enum for a publisher sample chapter / book reference.

groundingSources.title: Documenting Architecture Decisions (ADRs)
groundingSources.relevance: owns[3]: history-grounding mechanic — read ADRs to tell intentional constraint from accidental complexity before proposing change.
claimsSupported: ADRs record architecturally significant decisions affecting structure, non-functional characteristics, dependencies, interfaces, or construction techniques.
An ADR is a short text file with Title, Context, Decision, Status, Consequences.
groundingSources.notes: Coined by Nygard, 15 Nov 2011. The lesson's 'non-goals' emphasis is our reading of the Context/Consequences sections, not a Nygard field.

groundingSources.title: Technical Debt Quadrant
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Martin Fowler (quadrant); debt metaphor orig. Ward Cunningham
groundingSources.relevance: owns[3]: prioritization lens for ranking the 2-3 refactor candidates (pay down high-interest, prudent-deliberate debt first).
claimsSupported: Technical debt can be classified on deliberate/inadvertent x reckless/prudent axes.
The principal-vs-interest metaphor (Cunningham) frames shortcuts as debt whose interest is slowed future change.
groundingSources.notes: Primary sources fetched; specific framing not in the verified top-25. Bonus lens — re-confirm wording if quoted.

groundingSources.title: Exploring Generative AI (software delivery series)
groundingSources.relevance: Cross-cutting AI-assist spine: 'AI operationalizes the lens at scale; the human owns the decision' — the lesson's collaboration framing.
claimsSupported: Credible engineering framing for using GenAI in software delivery as an accelerant under human judgment.
groundingSources.notes: No adversarially-verified factual claim survived for the AI-assisted-refactoring dimension overall — use for framing/voice, not for efficacy claims. A dedicated grounding round is needed before any hard AI-refactoring claim.

unsupportedFacts: Feathers seam / characterization-test / sprout-wrap definitions: primary source fetched but wording not adversarially re-verified (medium) — re-confirm against the sample PDF.
Fitness functions (Ford/Parsons/Kua) and technical-debt quadrant (Fowler/Cunningham) framings: medium confidence, not in verified top-25.
AI-assisted-refactoring efficacy / failure-mode claims (2024-2026): no surviving verified claim — directional only, needs a dedicated grounding round.
needsHumanDecision: Brooks: original wording is 'essence'/'accidents'; treat 'essential/accidental complexity' as a popularized restatement.
Branch by Abstraction origin post is 2007, not 2004 (2004 = Strangler Fig) — keep the dates distinct.
ADR 'non-goals' is the lesson's extension of Nygard's Context/Consequences, not a Nygard term.
Mikado 'revert / resolve-leaves-first' loop is confirmed in substance, not a verbatim Manning quote.

lessons.title: Modernizacja legacy z DDD: wydzielaj domeny, potem deleguj Agentowi
lessons.titleEn: Legacy Modernization with DDD: Extract Domains, Then Delegate to the Agent
owns: Ubiquitous language: destylacja słownika domeny w dwóch trybach — symulowany wywiad z ekspertem domenowym (ręczny czat lub zautomatyzowany dwumodelowy wywiad narzędziem agent-forum z opcjonalnym summarizerem destylującym brief domenowy) oraz konfrontacja dokumentów kontekstowych z kodem gotowym promptem, którego produktem jest mapa rozjazdów model-vs-kod (artefakt 01-domain-distillation.md) — granice językowe ujawniają granice kontekstów (E. Evans)

Niezmiennik (invariant) → agregat-strażnik: jedno miejsce egzekwowania reguły z preconditions, nazwanymi błędami domenowymi i atomowością w jednej transakcji; gotowy prompt produkuje plan refaktoru (artefakt 02-invariant-aggregate-refactor.md) (E. Evans / V. Vernon)

Anti-Corruption Layer: value object + wąski port + adapter izolujący przeciekającą zależność, ze sprawdzalnym kryterium izolacji (grep po nazwie pakietu zwraca wyłącznie adapter); gotowy prompt produkuje plan refaktoru (artefakt 03-anti-corruption-layer.md) (E. Evans)

Event Storming z Agentem-moderatorem na wspólnym whiteboardzie event-storming-canvas (board.json jako jedyne źródło prawdy, edycja człowiek + Agent, odświeżanie przez SSE): zdarzenia → hotspoty jako kandydaci do dalszej analizy (A. Brandolini)

AI jako DDD-coach i handoff do drugiego cyklu „post-MVP”: artefakty DDD (context/domain/*, board.json) jako wsad do roadmapy i planowania przez /10x-shape, /10x-roadmap, /10x-research, /10x-plan — DDD zasila znaną pętlę, nie zastępuje jej
referencesOnly: Pełny katalog Context Mappingu poza ACL (Conformist, Open Host Service, Published Language, Shared Kernel, Customer/Supplier) — wolno nazwać lub odesłać do Deep Dive, nie uczymy (E. Evans; V. Vernon)
Destylacja subdomen Core / Supporting / Generic — pojawia się wyłącznie wewnątrz promptu destylacji, nie jako osobno nauczany temat
Encje vs value objects, reference-by-identity, eventual consistency, „jeden agregat na transakcję” (V. Vernon) — tło niezmienników i agregatów; głębiej w Deep Dive
Transactional Outbox (Richardson) — idea trwałego rekordu + przetwarzania out-of-band; uzasadnia async, nie implementujemy
Strangler Fig / Branch by Abstraction / Mikado (Fowler; owned by m4-l4) — wolno nazwać dla ciągłości, nie uczyć
10x-roadmap + cykl research → plan → implementacja (m2) — reużywane jako mechanizm drugiego cyklu, nie re-tłumaczone
mustNotCover: Pełna kodowa implementacja agregatu, ACL lub async pipeline w 10xCards — lekcja zostaje na poziomie promptu/planu; build jest delegowany do drugiego cyklu
Nauczanie strategii migracji (Strangler Fig, Branch by Abstraction, Mikado) — to m4-l4
Brokery (Kafka/RabbitMQ), event-sourcing, CQRS, mikroserwisy — poza zakresem
Agenci zespołowi / SDK / koszty / metryki — to m5-l1
SRS/ts-fsrs, auth, deck CRUD, rate-limit, GC jako tematy implementacyjne (mogą paść jako przykład przecieku lub hotspot, ale ich nie rozwiązujemy)
Pełny kurs i kanon DDD (agregaty na pięć warstw, spór o repozytoria) — celowo „tyle DDD, ile potrzeba”
learningOutcomes: Uczeń potrafi wydobyć i uspójnić ubiquitous language projektu, którego nie pisał, w dwóch trybach — symulowany wywiad z ekspertem domenowym oraz konfrontacja dokumentów kontekstowych z kodem — używając gotowego promptu do destylacji (produkt: mapa rozjazdów model-vs-kod, 01-domain-distillation.md)

Uczeń potrafi wskazać rdzeniowy niezmiennik domenowy i zaprojektować — gotowym promptem — jego egzekwowanie w jednym agregacie-strażniku (metody z preconditions, nazwane błędy domenowe, atomowość w jednej transakcji; 02-invariant-aggregate-refactor.md)
Uczeń potrafi zidentyfikować przeciekającą zależność zewnętrzną i zaprojektować Anti-Corruption Layer (value object + port + adapter) ze sprawdzalnym kryterium izolacji (03-anti-corruption-layer.md)

Uczeń potrafi przeprowadzić mini-warsztat Event Storming z Agentem-moderatorem na event-storming-canvas, wyprowadzić hotspoty i podać powstałe artefakty DDD jako wsad do drugiego cyklu „post-MVP” przez /10x-shape, /10x-roadmap, /10x-research, /10x-plan
videoPlaceholders: Wyniki trzech sesji DDD w 10xCards (ddd-prompts): prowadzący pokazuje artefakty z trzech promptów — destylacja języka (01-domain-distillation.md), niezmiennik→agregat (02-invariant-aggregate-refactor.md) i Anti-Corruption Layer (03-anti-corruption-layer.md) — oraz najważniejsze rozjazdy model-vs-kod.

Sesja Event Storming w event-storming-canvas (ddd-mentor): Agent w roli moderatora czyta i edytuje board.json na żywo — od fazy chaotic-exploration po hotspoty (błąd modelu, częściowo zaakceptowana sesja, timeout generacji); tablica odświeża się przez SSE.

groundingSources.title: Introducing Event Storming
groundingSources.relevance: Seminal post that names the Event Storming technique; establishes its origin and author.
claimsSupported: Event Storming originates with Alberto Brandolini; the seminal post is dated 18 Nov 2013.
A precursor 'Event-Based modelling workshop' was shown at Italian Agile Day 2012, renamed EventStorming in 2013.
groundingSources.notes: Cite the TECHNIQUE as 2013 (this blog). The book 'Introducing EventStorming' is a later, ongoing Leanpub work (~2018) — keep the two citations separate.

groundingSources.title: Introducing EventStorming (book)
groundingSources.relevance: Author's own book; source for the three levels and the workshop arc / sticky-note grammar.
claimsSupported: Three levels of Event Storming: Big Picture, Process, Software/Design.

Sticky grammar: domain events = orange (past tense), commands = blue (present tense), policies = lilac/purple, read models = green, external systems = pink, aggregates = yellow, hotspots = red/purple, pivotal events = timeline dividers.
groundingSources.notes: Book is ongoing/unfinished on Leanpub — no fixed publication year; cite as 'Leanpub, ongoing (~2018)'. Keep sticky-color claims above 'exact hex' (minor real-world variance).

groundingSources.title: Domain-Driven Design Reference: Definitions and Pattern Summaries
groundingSources.relevance: Evans' own canonical, free reference for strategic + tactical DDD; primary source for context mapping, subdomains, and entities/value objects.
claimsSupported: Canonical Context Mapping integration patterns: Partnership, Shared Kernel, Customer/Supplier, Conformist, Anticorruption Layer, Open Host Service, Published Language, Separate Ways, Big Ball of Mud (with upstream/downstream).
Subdomain distillation: Core / Supporting / Generic; invest in the Core Domain and justify the rest by how they support the distilled Core.
Entities (identity-based equality) vs Value Objects (value-based equality, immutability).
Linguistic boundaries reveal context boundaries (one ubiquitous language per bounded context).
groundingSources.notes: Pair with the books Evans 2003 (Domain-Driven Design) and Vernon 2013 (Implementing DDD). NOTE: the lesson 'owns' lists a 5-pattern subset (ACL, Conformist, OHS, Published Language, Shared Kernel) — the full canon adds Partnership, Customer/Supplier, Separate Ways, Big Ball of Mud.

groundingSources.title: Effective Aggregate Design (Parts I–III)
groundingSources.relevance: Primary source for aggregate/invariant rules; underwrites the niezmiennik→agregat-strażnik section (02-invariant-aggregate-refactor.md) and the DDD justification for the async (eventual-consistency) beat.
claimsSupported: Aggregates are consistency boundaries that protect invariants.
Reference other aggregates by identity, not by object reference.
Modify one aggregate per transaction; use eventual consistency between aggregates.
groundingSources.notes: Three-part series, 2011 (I: single aggregate, II: aggregates together, III: discovery).

groundingSources.title: Bounded Context Canvas
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Nick Tune & the DDD Crew (with Eric Evans)
groundingSources.relevance: Open-source capture template for a bounded context — optional named reference in Materiały dodatkowe for capturing a context after distillation.
claimsSupported: The canvas captures name/description, ubiquitous language, responsibilities, inbound/outbound messages (suppliers/consumers), message types (Command/Domain Event/Query), and dependencies.
groundingSources.notes: Attribute as 'Nick Tune & the DDD Crew (with Eric Evans)', maintained open-source — not Tune solo. Miro/draw.io/Excalidraw templates exist.

groundingSources.title: Pattern: Transactional outbox
groundingSources.relevance: Named pattern that justifies the Beat-2 worked example: durable record + out-of-band processing for the sync→async flashcard-generation refactor.
claimsSupported: One DB transaction updates business state and inserts the event into an OUTBOX table; a separate relay publishes it asynchronously, solving the dual-write problem without 2PC.
groundingSources.notes: Right-sizing caveat (honor notes §6): canonically a microservices/broker pattern. For 10xCards present it as the IDEA (durable record + out-of-band worker), implemented as a DB-backed job — NOT Kafka/RabbitMQ.

groundingSources.title: Strangler Fig Application / Branch by Abstraction
groundingSources.relevance: Incremental, reversible migration strategies referenced (not taught) by the L5 async refactor; primary ownership is m4-l4.
claimsSupported: Strangler Fig — incremental migration around a stable facade; original post 29 Jun 2004 (renamed from 'Strangler Application').
Branch by Abstraction — in-codebase equivalent: an abstraction layer lets old and new implementations coexist during migration.
groundingSources.notes: NEIGHBOR-OWNED: m4-l4 owns these strategies. In L5 they are reference-only (continuity), must not be re-taught — scope theft from L4 otherwise.

groundingSources.title: event-storming-canvas
groundingSources.relevance: Course-built dependency-free Node.js whiteboard demonstrated in Section 5; the canonical 'human + Agent share one live state' tool that replaced the earlier tldraw MCP / Mermaid idea for the Event Storming workshop.
claimsSupported: board.json is the single source of truth for the whole board; the agent reads and edits it ('thinks on the board') while the human edits in the browser.
The board refreshes live in every connected browser via Server-Sent Events; no npm install, no build step (pure Node.js, node server.js on :4000).

The tool encodes Brandolini's grammar (color = role) and a phase selector (chaotic-exploration → timeline → hotspots → aggregates) that locks the toolbar to the roles valid for the current phase; sessions are stateless, state lives only in board.json.
The repo's CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md ships the moderator protocol that turns a plain assistant into an Event Storming facilitator.
groundingSources.notes: Supersedes the earlier tldraw MCP App + tldraw Agent Starter Kit + Mermaid fallback idea (back-prop 2026-06-05). Appears in Materiały dodatkowe and powers the ddd-mentor video placeholder.

groundingSources.title: agent-forum
groundingSources.relevance: Course-built tool added to Section 2 ('Od ręcznego czatu do zautomatyzowanego wywiadu'): runs an automated turn-based interview between two models — one playing the developer, one the domain expert — to probe how well a model knows a chosen domain.
claimsSupported: In index.ts you define two agents (model + personality), the number of rounds, and a starting topic via a Forum({...}) config.
An optional summarizer distills the whole conversation into a ready domain brief (glossary, rules, pitfalls).
Run flow: clone repo, set OPENROUTER_API_KEY in .env (one key reaches many providers via OpenRouter), npm start; each turn is written as a markdown file under threads/, with a final summary.md.
Putting two models from different providers face to face doubles as a hallucination cross-check — agreement lowers risk, divergence flags claims to verify by hand.
groundingSources.notes: New in draft 2026-06-06. Linked inline in Section 2 and referenced again in Deep Dive 'Kiedy ekspert domenowy zmyśla' (two-models cross-check). Not yet in the draft's Materiały dodatkowe list — author decision pending whether to add it there.

lessons.title: AI Internal Builders: wewnętrzne narzędzia, serwisy i automatyzacje
lessons.titleEn: AI Internal Builders: internal tools, services and automations
owns: Build-vs-buy-vs-complement decision frame for internal AI tools: generic/utility workflows default to SaaS, local cross-system friction can justify a thin internal helper, durable proven helpers can graduate into later Module 5 implementation paths.

SaaS as platform responsibility, not a feature checklist: security, compliance, audits, permissions, uptime, procurement, support, vendor risk, identity/tenant concerns, and operations are part of what the vendor platform carries.

Team friction as the signal for internal-builder opportunities: repeated coordination cost visible across GitHub, Linear/Jira, CI, incidents, support threads, release history, or internal company DB signals.
Opportunity map artifact: friction signal -> SaaS/default response -> internal helper -> first useful version -> later M5 path.
First useful version rule: start as a narrow read-only helper around existing systems before productizing, deploying, or adding auth/backlog ownership.
Internal builder leverage: trust and influence from reducing visible team pain, without promising promotion or formal career outcomes.

10xChampion evidence package: screenshot-based proof of a Module 5 team workflow, especially a CI/CD code-review pipeline or Shared AI Registry flow, so learners can use company context without exposing repositories.
referencesOnly: Agent/harness/tool-use definitions — prework [1.2].
IDE/terminal/cloud tool tradeoffs — prework [2.x].
Prompt/context/model discipline — prework [3.x].
CI/CD maturity baseline — prework [4.2].
The Mom Test / behavior-based feedback collection — 10xDevs 2 [5x5] and Opanuj Frontend AI Edition; m5-l1 uses it only as a validation checkpoint, not a full customer-discovery curriculum.
SDK agent implementation, costs, privacy, metrics — m5-l2.
PR review gates, Definition of Done, GitHub Actions, and code-review pipeline implementation — m5-l3.
Shared AI Registry, package distribution, release mechanics, and team artifact delivery — m5-l4.
Remote/async execution — m5-l5.
Concrete 10xChampion implementation artifacts from later lessons: code-review pipeline from m5-l2/m5-l3 or shared AI registry from m5-l4.
mustNotCover: How to build an agent with an SDK.
How to run a review agent in CI.
How to distribute skills, commands, rules, packages, registries, or CLI delivery.
How to configure remote agents, cloud sandboxes, loops, or async execution.
How to replace full SaaS products or rebuild their security/compliance/audit responsibilities.
Full BI/dashboard design, analytics engineering, data modeling, or product discovery.
General career advice or claims that internal tools guarantee promotion.
Exact implementation of the 10xChampion submission workflow; this lesson introduces requirements and evidence shape only.
learningOutcomes: Learner can distinguish a workflow that should be bought from local friction worth addressing internally.
Learner can explain why internal building usually means complementing SaaS, not replacing the vendor platform.
Learner can classify recurring team pain with an opportunity map and route it to the right later M5 path.
Learner can define a first useful internal-helper version that avoids unnecessary deployment, auth, and productization.
Learner can explain why AI lowers the cost of small bespoke helpers without making every annoyance worth building.
Learner can name how solving visible team friction creates trust and influence without treating promotion as guaranteed.
Learner can explain what evidence the 10xChampion badge expects: screenshot-based proof of a team workflow, without requiring a public repository link.
requiredFragments: Generic company running scenario: GitHub + Linear/Jira + CI + internal company DB as normal data surfaces that can feed a read-only triage digest.
Opportunity map as the required decision artifact: friction signal -> SaaS/default response -> internal helper -> first useful version -> later M5 path.

Sensitive-data caveat kept practical and non-alarmist: mocked/local/read-only/non-sensitive data can stay lightweight; real company/customer data requires access-control and auditability thinking earlier.

Mom Test validation checkpoint after opportunity map / shape / PRD: use behavior-based questions about recent situations, current workarounds, and real cost of friction; ship `/10x-mom-test` to produce interview and survey questions.
10xChampion badge path introduced in the practical-task section: Module 5 is optional beyond the base Builder certificate; evidence is screenshots rather than repository links.
10xChampion evidence checklist: list of releases, repository where the workflow exists, package definition, at least one job, pipeline view, and logs.
Valid 10xChampion paths: CI/CD code-review pipeline built across m5-l2/m5-l3, or Shared AI Registry/team artifact distribution built in m5-l4.
videoPlaceholders: Decision walkthrough: classify 3 prepared scenarios in the opportunity map and explain why one is buy, one is complement, and one should wait.

Tiny artifact demo: show a mocked/read-only triage digest generated from normal GitHub, Linear/Jira, CI, and internal-company-DB signals. The video must not show SDK code, CI implementation, auth, deployment, registry, or remote-agent setup.
10xChampion evidence walkthrough: show the expected screenshot categories at a high level, without implementing the pipeline or registry in this lesson.

groundingSources.title: Utility Vs Strategic Dichotomy
groundingSources.relevance: Durable build/buy framing for utility versus strategic software; supports the lesson's buy/build/complement decision frame without turning every internal annoyance into a custom project.
claimsSupported: Utility capabilities are usually better handled by packages or SaaS, while strategic differentiation can justify custom software
Buying a package and then heavily customizing it can waste effort in a different way than rebuilding from scratch
groundingSources.notes: Use as framing, not as an AI-specific source.

groundingSources.title: NIST CSRC Glossary — Software as a Service (SaaS)
groundingSources.relevance: Grounds SaaS as provider-hosted applications where the consumer generally does not manage infrastructure or full application capabilities.
claimsSupported: SaaS is a provider application running on cloud infrastructure rather than just a visible feature set
Consumers usually manage limited configuration rather than underlying infrastructure or full application capabilities
groundingSources.notes: Keep learner-facing prose practical; do not turn the section into cloud-service taxonomy.

groundingSources.title: AWS Well-Architected SaaS Lens
groundingSources.relevance: Supports the invisible platform-responsibility guardrail: SaaS design spans operational excellence, security, reliability, tenant identity/isolation, onboarding, tiers, and tenant-aware operations.
claimsSupported: SaaS workloads require architectural and operational responsibilities beyond isolated feature delivery
Multi-tenant SaaS introduces security, operational, stability, identity, isolation, onboarding, and tenant-aware operation concerns
groundingSources.notes: AWS-specific but useful for naming invisible platform work.

groundingSources.title: Building effective agents
groundingSources.relevance: Grounds the first-useful-version rule: start simple, understand the underlying code, and avoid agentic/framework complexity when a simpler workflow is enough.
claimsSupported: Developers should start with the simplest useful LLM/API workflow and add complexity only when needed
Frameworks and agentic systems can obscure prompts/responses and tempt teams into complexity that is harder to debug
groundingSources.notes: Detailed SDK construction remains m5-l2.

groundingSources.title: Writing effective tools for AI agents — using AI agents
groundingSources.relevance: Supports the idea that repeated tool/workflow traces can reveal opportunities for consolidation, while still requiring metrics and human judgment.
claimsSupported: Tracking runtime, tool calls, token consumption, and tool errors can reveal common workflows and opportunities to consolidate tools
Agents can help identify tool issues but omissions and failures still matter
groundingSources.notes: Use as supporting material for recurring-friction signals, not as a broad internal-tools strategy source.

groundingSources.title: The State of the Art in End-User Software Engineering
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Andrew J. Ko et al. / ACM Computing Surveys
groundingSources.relevance: Grounds the risk that small internal or end-user-created tools often keep requirements, reuse, testing, verification, debugging, and maintenance implicit unless deliberately constrained.
claimsSupported: End-user/internal software is often built to support another goal, making quality activities secondary or implicit
Small helpers that become critical need explicit ownership, quality, and maintenance decisions
groundingSources.notes: Use insights silently or in Deep Dive; avoid academic terminology in the main lesson.

groundingSources.title: Identification of Coordination Requirements: Implications for the Design of Collaboration and Awareness Tools
groundingSources.publisherOrAuthor: Marcelo Cataldo, Patrick A. Wagstrom, James D. Herbsleb, Kathleen M. Carley / CSCW 2006
groundingSources.relevance: Grounds team friction as a real software-engineering signal visible in archival traces and coordination patterns.
claimsSupported: Coordination requirements can be inferred from development traces and can cross team boundaries
Better congruence between coordination needs and coordination activity shortened development time in the studied project
groundingSources.notes: Do not overclaim that the course triage digest itself proves productivity gains.

groundingSources.title: GitHub Blog — Building AI-powered GitHub issue triage with the Copilot SDK
groundingSources.relevance: Current concrete example of AI-assisted issue summaries/triage and a reminder that even small helpers require architecture and credential handling.
claimsSupported: AI issue summaries can reduce triage reading/context-switching for active repositories
A small triage helper may still need server-side runtime and secret-handling decisions
groundingSources.notes: Recheck SDK mechanics before quoting; m5-l1 should keep the demo mocked/read-only.

groundingSources.title: Linear Docs — Triage Intelligence and issue discussion summaries
groundingSources.relevance: Current SaaS-native AI example: triage suggestions and discussion summaries; supports the buy/default side of the opportunity map.
claimsSupported: Linear can use agentic models to suggest issue properties and relationships during triage
Linear issue discussion summaries condense decisions, blockers, debates, resolutions, and people involved
groundingSources.notes: Plan-tier and feature availability can change; keep prose generic and complement-focused.

newClaimsIntroduced: Grounding supports the build/buy/complement frame with utility-vs-strategic framing, SaaS definitions, SaaS architecture responsibility sources, coordination research, and current AI triage examples
User decision 2026-06-11: running example is a generic company scenario combining GitHub, Linear/Jira, CI, and internal company DB signals rather than a 10xCards-specific scenario.
User decision 2026-06-11: m5-l1 introduces 10xChampion screenshot-based evidence requirements for Module 5 team workflows.
User decision 2026-06-13: m5-l1 brings back The Mom Test from 10xDevs 2 / Opanuj Frontend as a lightweight validation checkpoint and ships `/10x-mom-test` for interview and survey design.

potentialDuplicates: m5-l2 if the triage digest becomes an SDK build instead of a decision/read-only example
m5-l3 if the lesson teaches CI review gates or PR pipeline mechanics
m5-l4 if the lesson teaches shared skill/rule/package distribution instead of only routing to that later path

lessons.title: Twój pierwszy Agent zespołowy: SDK, koszty, metryki
lessons.titleEn: Your first Team Agent: SDK, costs, metrics

groundingSources.title: Claude Agent SDK — Overview & TypeScript reference
groundingSources.relevance: Ready-made-agent-SDK half of the two-SDK build: built-in tools, query() loop in-process, systemPrompt persona, native outputFormat json_schema structured output, maxTurns.
claimsSupported: Claude Agent SDK is a ready-made-agent SDK that runs the agent loop in your own process on your filesystem
Ships 14+ built-in tools; systemPrompt defines the agent's persona; native structured output via outputFormat json_schema
Renamed Claude Code SDK -> Claude Agent SDK in Sept 2025; TS package bundles a native binary
groundingSources.notes: TS V2 preview (send()/stream()) is a preview surface — do not teach as stable. Full detail in claude-sdk.notes.md.

groundingSources.title: AI SDK 6 — announcement, Building Agents, Loop Control
groundingSources.relevance: Assemble-it-yourself-SDK half of the two-SDK build: ToolLoopAgent, Output.object structured output with Zod, stopWhen, direct-to-provider with optional AI Gateway.
claimsSupported: AI SDK 6 is an assemble-it-yourself SDK: you bring the tools, the SDK runs the model->tool loop
ToolLoopAgent implements the Agent interface; stopWhen default stepCountIs(20); token/cost ceilings need a custom StopCondition
License Apache-2.0; stable since 2025-12-22
groundingSources.notes: v7 already in canary — teach v6 stable. Full detail in vercel-ai-sdk.notes.md.

groundingSources.title: Codex SDK — Overview & TypeScript README
groundingSources.relevance: Deep-dive decision-table row: ready-made-agent SDK that wraps the codex CLI over JSONL, per-turn sandbox presets, lockstep versioning.
claimsSupported: Codex SDK wraps the codex CLI as a subprocess exchanging JSONL over stdio; needs the CLI runtime present
Per-turn sandbox presets (read_only / workspace_write / full_access); first-class outputSchema; lockstep version pin to @openai/codex
groundingSources.notes: workingDirectory must be a Git repo unless skipGitRepoCheck. Full detail in codex-sdk.notes.md.

groundingSources.title: Cursor SDK — official blog, changelog, Cloud Agents API docs
groundingSources.relevance: Deep-dive decision-table row: ready-made-agent-as-infrastructure — durable agents, cloud/self-host/local runtimes, codebase indexing, IDE takeover.
claimsSupported: Cursor SDK turns the interactive agent into headless infrastructure: durable Agent.create + per-prompt runs, three runtimes
Codebase indexing + semantic search baked in; public beta released Apr 28 2026
groundingSources.notes: Public beta — pin versions; event payloads/option types/model IDs may shift. Structured output is NOT a primary feature — do not claim it. Full detail in cursor-sdk.notes.md.

groundingSources.title: OpenRouter Agent SDK — Overview & Call Model reference
groundingSources.relevance: Deep-dive row + the strongest cost-ceiling primitive for the costs/metrics section: callModel loop, 400+ models, fallback routing, maxCost.
claimsSupported: OpenRouter Agent SDK is a gateway-first assemble-it-yourself SDK: one callModel over 400+ models with fallback routing
Richest built-in stop conditions including maxCost(amount), maxTokensUsed(n), finishReasonIs(reason)
groundingSources.notes: It is OpenRouter's SDK (one fetched page misread it as Anthropic's). Verify @openrouter/agent vs @openrouter/sdk and input: vs messages: before quoting syntax. Full detail in openrouter-agent-sdk.notes.md.

groundingSources.title: Agent SDK Comparison — Claude / Codex / Cursor / OpenRouter / Vercel (internal synthesis)
groundingSources.relevance: The ready-made-vs-assemble-it-yourself framing and the at-a-glance 5-SDK matrix that the lesson's tight decision table compresses.
claimsSupported: Agent SDKs split into ready-made-agent SDKs and assemble-it-yourself SDKs; almost every other difference follows from that category
Per-SDK strengths/weaknesses condensed into a decision table
groundingSources.notes: Synthesis of the per-SDK notes; beta surfaces flagged inside.

groundingSources.title: Agent SDK Privacy, Data & Pricing — cross-SDK summary (internal synthesis)
groundingSources.relevance: Privacy beat (auth-path rule) and the operational costs/metrics section (pricing, cost ceilings, June 15 2026 billing split).
claimsSupported: No SDK has its own privacy/billing regime; the auth path you choose decides training/retention/residency
June 15 2026: programmatic Claude usage on subscription plans draws a separate monthly Agent SDK credit (Help Center #15036540)
Cost-ceiling primitives differ by SDK (OpenRouter maxCost vs AI SDK custom StopCondition)
groundingSources.notes: Pricing is fast-moving — frame as 'as of mid-2026'. A few minor [unverified] items remain (see that file's ledger). Keep to LOCAL execution; CI/observability is m5-l3.

groundingSources.title: Claude Agent SDK — single-file local code-review agent (internal walkthrough)
groundingSources.relevance: The lesson's spine artifact (harness build): empty repo -> one review.ts that takes a git diff and returns a structured JSON review, run locally.
claimsSupported: A minimal local code-review agent (diff -> structured JSON review) can be built in a single file on the Claude Agent SDK
Each knob (systemPrompt, outputFormat, tools: [], maxTurns) maps to 'what the harness gave me'
groundingSources.notes: Pairs with the AI SDK 6 walkthrough for the 'same scenario, two SDKs' beat. Sibling Cursor/OpenRouter walkthroughs also exist.

groundingSources.title: AI SDK 6 — single-file local code-review agent (internal walkthrough)
groundingSources.relevance: The loop-primitive half of the spine: the same diff -> structured JSON review built on AI SDK 6, run locally. API verified via Context7 + ai-sdk.dev.
claimsSupported: The same code-review agent is assembled from named primitives on AI SDK 6: ToolLoopAgent + tool() + Output.object({ schema }) + your own provider import
generate() returns { output } typed/validated by the Zod schema; structured output costs an extra step; tools is a record; inputSchema not parameters
Model choice is an explicit swappable input (anthropic('claude-sonnet-4-6') direct vs 'anthropic/...' gateway string)
groundingSources.notes: Same JSON out as the Claude build — the SDK category is the only variable. Written 2026-06-08 against verified AI SDK 6 docs.

groundingSources.title: promptfoo — Claude Agent SDK & Codex SDK providers + custom JS provider (evals)
groundingSources.relevance: Grounds the systemic-improvement beat: wrap the same review agent in a local eval set (the quality counterpart to operational metrics).
claimsSupported: promptfoo has a first-class anthropic:claude-agent-sdk provider (alias anthropic:claude-code) with working_dir/model/permission_mode/append_allowed_tools/max_turns/output_format config
AI SDK 6 has no dedicated provider — wrap it with the file:// custom JS provider (ApiProvider.callApi returning { output })
One eval set + is-json/javascript/llm-rubric assertions runs against both builds locally via 'promptfoo eval'
groundingSources.notes: Verified 2026-06-08 (Context7 /websites/promptfoo_dev + official docs). Keep prose concept-level. The promptfoo-action (PR before/after in CI) is m5-l3 territory — forward-reference only.

unsupportedFacts: Per-SDK pricing numbers are dated 2026-06-08 and fast-moving — frame as 'as of mid-2026', re-verify before publish
Beta/preview surfaces (Cursor SDK public beta, Claude TS V2 preview, AI SDK v7 canary) may shift — pin versions
needsHumanDecision: Re-verify the June 15 2026 Claude billing credit amounts/mechanics against Anthropic Help Center #15036540 at publish time

lessons.title: Code Review w erze AI: standardy, DoD i Agent w pipeline
lessons.titleEn: Code Review in the Age of AI: Standards, DoDs and Agents in the Pipeline
owns: Shipping a self-built code-review agent from localhost into a GitHub Actions PR pipeline: the minimum GHA vocabulary (workflow, trigger, job, step, action, runner) and a minimal pull_request + workflow_dispatch review workflow.
Passing the LLM provider key from GitHub repository secrets into the review step (the ephemeral-runner model; the key is not on the runner).

Packaging the agent as a reusable Composite Action: action.yml with using: composite, inputs/outputs, github.action_path, per-step shell; local-subfolder vs separate-repo hosting; pinning third-party actions to a commit SHA.

Treating the CI/CD workflow as a normal feature built with the 10x research -> plan -> implement flow, seeded by a requirements.md brainstorm that scopes an MVP review contract (input params, 1-10 review criteria, side-effect labels ai-cr:passed/ai-cr:failed, on-demand retry label).

Operationalizing promptfoo as a model-comparison + regression gate in CI: promptfooconfig.yaml (prompts/providers/tests), OpenRouter as a single-key multi-vendor provider, is-json/llm-rubric/javascript assertions, the result matrix, and PROMPTFOO_PASS_RATE_THRESHOLD.

The tool loop as the path from a one-shot scorer to an agent: ToolLoopAgent + Output.object vs adding tools (tool() = description/inputSchema/execute), the validate -> execute -> continue loop, step-limit safety (stopWhen: stepCountIs, the runaway-loop / isLoopFinished cost risk, onStepFinish token telemetry), and the read -> write -> whole-GitHub -> external ladder of agency.

10x-impl-review-ci as the production reference, read through its two-layer split (SKILL.md run-mechanics vs references/impl-review-instructions.md portable criteria) and why an auto-loaded skill self-structures a prose verdict while a self-built SDK agent enforces a schema.

Deep Dive: scaling AI review across a team (throughput-not-replacement, risk-based triage, cost/time as a metric via onStepFinish, the human-override / bug-on-green learning loop feeding the review criteria and the promptfoo set).
referencesOnly: Building the local agent from SDK primitives (Claude Agent SDK / AI SDK 6) — owned by m5-l2; here it is the artifact the CI calls and the class the tools attach to.
promptfoo as a concept — introduced in m5-l2; here operationalized in CI, not re-introduced.
Cost/privacy of the self-built agent — m5-l2; one callback only: the auth path is now the CI's secret.
Authoring & distributing team standards / a shared registry — m5-l4; standards appear here only as the input the agent enforces via review criteria.
harness/agent model — prework [1.2]; CI/CD as a project requirement — prework [4.2]; model tiers — prework [3.5].
mustNotCover: Authoring or distributing the team's shared standards/skills/rules (m5-l4).
Building the local agent from SDK primitives (m5-l2).
Async & remote agents (m5-l5).
A general GitHub Actions / CI-CD course beyond what is needed to run the agent (incl. OIDC and the full ruleset / branch-protection UI).
Re-introducing promptfoo from scratch (m5-l2).
A claude-code-action / Claude Code Skills tutorial — the Deep Dive uses the production skill as contrast + adoption, not a feature tour.
learningOutcomes: Wire a self-built code-review agent into a GitHub Actions workflow triggered on a pull request (plus manual workflow_dispatch), passing the provider key from repository secrets into the step.
Map the core GHA vocabulary (workflow, trigger, job, step, action, runner) onto a real review workflow file.
Package the agent as a reusable Composite Action and consume it from a one-step workflow, pinning third-party actions to a commit SHA.
Drive the CI/CD build with the 10x research -> plan -> implement flow seeded by a requirements.md brainstorm, scoping an MVP review contract (criteria, labels, retry).
Use promptfoo as a CI gate to compare models across vendors via OpenRouter and catch silent prompt regressions, instead of judging a change on a single PR.
Extend the agent from a one-shot scorer to a tool-using actor by adding tools to the same ToolLoopAgent, keeping the loop safe with a hard step limit and reading token cost from onStepFinish.
Place a new tool on the read -> write -> whole-GitHub -> external ladder of agency and choose read-only first.
Read 10x-impl-review-ci as a production reference, explaining its two-layer split and why an auto-loaded skill self-structures a prose verdict while a self-built agent enforces a schema.
Design a risk-based triage + metrics + learning-loop scheme for scaling AI review across a team without removing humans from critical paths.
videoPlaceholders: Course video-scenario (requiredArtifact), embedded after the 'Integracja Github Actions z Agentem do Code Review' section (Vimeo 1200973339): the canonical flow on the self-built agent — open a PR with a planted SQL injection, the GHA workflow runs the agent, ai-cr:failed label + red ai-cr/verdict status land and block the merge; fix the code and add tests, push, the verdict flips to ai-cr:passed/green and the merge unblocks. The status-check + gh-api gate mechanics live in the video; the draft prose mentions the merge gate only in passing. The 10x-impl-review-ci reveal stays text-only (Deep Dive). Validated end-to-end 2026-06-10 on przeprogramowani/10xCards PR #19 (fail: score 1.4 -> fix: score 4.6).

Inline VIDEO PLACEHOLDER in the promptfoo section: promptfoo as an anti-optimization-carousel — a short promptfooconfig.yaml with two-three OpenRouter models on the same diff set, npx promptfoo eval, walking the result matrix (pass/fail + cost + time), showing a silent regression after a prompt edit, and how the same eval blocks merge as a CI step.

groundingSources.title: GitHub Actions — workflow syntax, pull_request triggers, concurrency
groundingSources.relevance: The 'minimum GHA to run an agent on a PR' beat: .github/workflows/*.yml, on: pull_request default types (opened/synchronize/reopened), jobs/runs-on/steps, uses vs run, checkout, and the canonical PR concurrency group with cancel-in-progress.
claimsSupported: A workflow file under .github/workflows runs a job on a pull request; default pull_request activity types are opened/synchronize/reopened, and synchronize fires on every push to the PR head.
A step is either uses: (an action) or run: (shell); actions/checkout must precede any run: that touches repo files.
concurrency group + cancel-in-progress cancels the stale run when a new commit is pushed.
groundingSources.notes: VERSION-SENSITIVE: actions/checkout and actions/setup-node are at @v6 (Node 24 runtime since 2026-06-02), not @v4. The 10x-impl-review-ci workflow-template still shows @v4 (works but stale). Re-verify majors at publish; don't cite action release dates.

groundingSources.title: GitHub Actions — using secrets, GITHUB_TOKEN, permissions
groundingSources.relevance: The 'key isn't on the runner' beat: repo secret in the UI -> ${{ secrets.X }} -> env: -> process.env; ephemeral runner; best-effort masking; least-privilege permissions; no secrets on fork PRs.
claimsSupported: Repo secrets are set in Settings -> Secrets and variables -> Actions, referenced as ${{ secrets.NAME }}, exposed via env: and read as process.env.NAME.
Each job runs on a fresh VM with the secret injected at runtime and destroyed after; log masking is best-effort, not guaranteed.
Declaring any permissions: block sets unlisted scopes to none; statuses: write must be declared explicitly to POST a commit status.
Except GITHUB_TOKEN (read-only on forks), secrets are not passed to pull_request workflows from forks; pull_request_target + checkout of PR head is a secret-exfiltration footgun.
groundingSources.notes: Related pages: concepts/security/github_token and controlling-permissions-for-github_token. Couldn't pull a verbatim line for the default statuses level under the restricted token — safe guidance: always declare permissions: { statuses: write } explicitly.

groundingSources.title: GitHub Actions — Creating a composite action
groundingSources.relevance: The 'Composite Action: agent jako reużywalny plugin' section: packaging the agent's steps into a reusable action.yml so one agent can be wired into many repos with a single line.
claimsSupported: A composite action is defined by action.yml with runs.using: composite; it has inputs and outputs, does NOT declare its own trigger or runner (the consumer's workflow does), and is injected into the consumer workflow as steps.

Each run: step inside a composite action must set shell explicitly (unlike a normal workflow where shell defaults); ${{ github.action_path }} points at the action's directory so it can run any script bundled with it.

Hosting: a separate repo (uses: org/action@<sha>, publishable to the Marketplace) or a local subfolder .github/actions/<name>/ (uses: ./...); the consumer workflow shrinks to checkout + one uses: step.
groundingSources.notes: Stable, long-standing GitHub feature; claims are well within current behavior. The draft pairs this with the supply-chain rule to pin third-party actions to a commit SHA (@<sha>), not a moving tag (@v1) — that rule is grounded in the GitHub security guidance generally, not this page specifically.

groundingSources.title: GitHub — required status checks, branch protection, rulesets (the merge gate)
groundingSources.relevance: Backs the merge-gate mechanics shown in the video scenario and behind the draft's passing 'bramka, która zatrzyma merge' mentions: a label never blocks a merge; a required status check (e.g. ai-cr/verdict posted to the PR HEAD SHA) does. The shipped draft keeps labels as the visible verdict and defers the status-check + gh-api wiring to the video, so this source supports the video more than the prose.
claimsSupported: GitHub has no built-in 'block on label' merge rule; the enforced gate is a required status check selected by name in branch protection or a ruleset, blocking while it is failure/error/pending (success/skipped/neutral unblock).
Two underlying APIs can be required checks: the Commit Status API (POST /repos/{o}/{r}/statuses/{sha}, state + context string) and the Checks API (check runs, GitHub Apps only).
The 'post a status to HEAD SHA' pattern: workflow posts a commit status with a stable context (e.g. ai-cr:verdict) to the PR HEAD and branch protection requires it; needs statuses: write.

A bot commit with [skip ci] plus GITHUB_TOKEN's no-retrigger guard means no new auto check lands on the new HEAD, so the required check sits pending forever — hence POST a manual status to the real HEAD SHA.

Rulesets are the current recommended system (additive, multiple apply, evaluate mode) and coexist with classic branch protection; required checks can be pinned to an expected GitHub App to prevent spoofed success.
groundingSources.notes: Companion pages: managing-rulesets/available-rules-for-rulesets, managing-protected-branches/about-protected-branches, rest/commits/statuses, managing-workflow-runs/skipping-workflow-runs. Independently confirmed by the 10x-impl-review-ci verdict-check step (impl-review-ci/verdict status + impl-review-override label).

groundingSources.title: Anthropic — structured outputs (DoD as an executable schema)
groundingSources.relevance: The self-built structuring path the draft leans on lightly: a fuzzy review becomes deterministic pass/fail because the criteria are a Zod/JSON schema the model must fill, with a threshold on a score field. In the shipped draft this appears via Output.object (the scorer in the tool-loop section) and as the contrast in the Deep Dive two-layer-skill discussion — it is NOT a dedicated 'DoD as a Zod schema' section.
claimsSupported: Structured output is a feature of POST /v1/messages via output_config.format ({ type: 'json_schema', schema }); client.messages.parse() + zodOutputFormat(ZodSchema) is the TS Zod path; parsed_output carries the result.

JSON-schema support requires additionalProperties:false on every object and does NOT support recursive schemas or numeric/string-length constraints (SDKs strip + validate client-side); supported on Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 4.6 / Haiku 4.5.
groundingSources.notes: VERSION-SENSITIVE: the old top-level output_format param is deprecated — use output_config.format. AI SDK 6's equivalent (Output.object + ToolLoopAgent) is already grounded in m5-l2; reference it, keep concept-level.

groundingSources.title: Vercel AI SDK (v6) — Tools and tool calling / agents (ToolLoopAgent)
groundingSources.relevance: The 'Więcej możliwości dla agenta' section: turning the one-shot scorer into a tool-using agent on the same ToolLoopAgent class, and the read -> write -> whole-GitHub -> external ladder of agency.
claimsSupported: tool() is description + inputSchema (Zod, used both to tell the model the input shape and to validate what it generates) + execute (async, returns the result to the model); registering it is one field, tools, on the same ToolLoopAgent.

ToolLoopAgent({ model, instructions, output: Output.object({ schema }) }) is the scorer form; adding tools turns it into a loop where the model either emits the final answer or requests a tool, the SDK validates args against inputSchema, runs execute, appends the result, and continues.
Loop safety: stopWhen: stepCountIs(n) caps the session; onStepFinish exposes per-step token usage; isLoopFinished removes the step limit (a cost risk on CI, multiplied per PR).
groundingSources.notes: Verified against /vercel/ai @ ai_6.0.0-beta.128 (2026-06-14): tool() shape, ToolLoopAgent({ model, instructions, tools, stopWhen }), stopWhen: stepCountIs(n), and Output.object({ schema }) all confirmed. CAVEAT — the draft states the DEFAULT is 'stops after 20 steps (stopWhen: stepCountIs(20))'; docs show stepCountIs(20) as the canonical example but the default value when stopWhen is omitted was not confirmed from primary source. Re-verify the default at publish. onStepFinish / isLoopFinished names per draft; confirm exact spelling at publish.

groundingSources.title: promptfoo — CI/CD integration + GitHub Action
groundingSources.relevance: Beat 6 (who reviews the reviewer): operationalizing promptfoo as a regression gate in CI — exit codes, thresholds, the official action. The CONCEPT is owned by m5-l2; only the CI operationalization is grounded here.
claimsSupported: promptfoo eval exits 100 when >=1 test fails or pass-rate < PROMPTFOO_PASS_RATE_THRESHOLD (default 100%), so a failing eval fails the CI job by default with no special flag.

promptfoo/promptfoo-action@v1 runs the eval on a PR and posts a pass/fail summary; key inputs config, github-token, anthropic-api-key, fail-on-threshold, repeat + repeat-min-pass, promptfoo-version; to block merge make it a required status check.
A self-built agent is wrapped via a file:// custom JS provider exporting callApi; the first-class anthropic:claude-agent-sdk provider also exists.
groundingSources.notes: VERSION-SENSITIVE: tutorials mention a --ci flag that current official CLI docs do NOT document — non-zero (100) exit is the default; don't teach --ci. Hosted runners don't persist ~/.cache/promptfoo (add actions/cache@v4). Companion: integrations/github-action, usage/command-line.

groundingSources.title: promptfoo — assertions / expected outputs
groundingSources.relevance: Concrete eval cases for a code-review agent's structured output (task c: one regression-catching case).
claimsSupported: is-json (optional value = a JSON Schema validates the DoD shape), javascript/python (required keys, score bounds), llm-rubric (judge the free-form comment), contains/equals.
defaultTest.assert applies invariants to every test case; scoring uses assertion-level threshold + weight -> weighted average, test-level threshold, and assert-set threshold (proportion passing).
groundingSources.notes: Keep prose concept-level (promptfoo was introduced in m5-l2); the config is real, not hand-waved.

groundingSources.title: OpenRouter — Quickstart
groundingSources.relevance: The promptfoo section uses OpenRouter as the providers backend so the 'cheaper or pricier, maybe a different vendor' carousel is resolved with one API key instead of juggling several vendor accounts.
claimsSupported: One OPENROUTER_API_KEY gives access to models from many vendors; in promptfoo a provider is id: openrouter:<vendor>/<model> (e.g. openrouter:anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5, openrouter:openai/gpt-5-mini).
groundingSources.notes: Single-key multi-vendor access and the openrouter: prefix are stable; the specific model IDs in the draft (claude-haiku-4.5, claude-sonnet-4.6, gpt-5-mini) are current-not-perennial — re-verify available model slugs at publish.

groundingSources.title: anthropics/claude-code-action (v1) + Claude Code Skills auto-load
groundingSources.relevance: Deep Dive 'Dwie warstwy skilla 10x-impl-review-ci' contrast: WHY the production reference looks different — the action auto-loads a SKILL.md into an agent framework and the model self-structures a prose verdict, vs a self-built SDK agent that has no skill auto-load and enforces structure with a schema (Output.object).
claimsSupported: claude-code-action@v1 runs Claude Code (loads CLAUDE.md/.claude/skills/MCP) with inputs anthropic_api_key, prompt, and claude_args (--model/--max-turns/--allowedTools); top-level allowed_tools/max_turns/model were removed in v1.

It treats the PR head as untrusted: SENSITIVE_PATHS (.claude, CLAUDE.md, .mcp.json, .husky, ...) are quarantined to .claude-pr/ and restored from the base branch — so a review skill must already live on the base branch (or be staged user-level into ~/.claude/skills/).

Skill auto-load is a property of the runtime/harness (Claude Code / claude-code-action / Managed Agents), NOT of the model or an arbitrary SDK; a from-scratch SDK agent injects criteria via system prompt or diff-as-param and enforces structure with a Zod/JSON schema.
Inline comments via mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment (confirmed:true posts immediately; otherwise buffered + classified).
groundingSources.notes: Companion: code.claude.com/docs/en/skills (frontmatter name/description/allowed-tools; progressive disclosure). The buffering classifier model string is unpinned ('lightweight'). SENSITIVE_PATHS misses nested CLAUDE.md (issue #1270). Beat 7 = contrast + adoption, not a tutorial.

groundingSources.title: 10x-impl-review-ci — the production reference (SKILL.md + workflow-template.yml)
groundingSources.relevance: Deep Dive primary artifact: read through its TWO LAYERS — SKILL.md (run-mechanics: find the plan, compute the diff, split into subagents, write the report, commit with [skip ci], post the verdict status check) vs references/impl-review-instructions.md (portable criteria: plan as source of truth, three analyses, seven graded dimensions, severity/impact finding grammar). What it ADDS over the hand-rolled agent (plan-drift, test coverage, inline comments, REJECTED gate) and what it ASSUMES (a plan-driven context/changes/<id>/plan.md workflow). Read, not built. Delivered to learners via npx @przeprogramowani/10x-cli@latest get, invoked /10x-impl-review-ci.
claimsSupported: It is split into two layers with different lifespans: SKILL.md holds the run-mechanics tied to Claude Code (subagents, tasks, MCP inline comments, the [skip ci] commit, the status check) and points at references/impl-review-instructions.md, which holds only the portable evaluation criteria (plan as source of truth, three analyses, seven dimensions, finding grammar) and says nothing about where the plan/diff come from or where the result goes.

It compares the PR against the plan it claims to implement (drift/test-coverage/seven dimensions) and emits a committed report + inline comments + summary comment; no plan -> neutral comment + exit 0.

Why it skips Zod: it runs inside claude-code-action (auto-loaded SKILL.md), the model emits a markdown report, and the verdict is extracted by a shell grep of the 'Verdict:' line — the output contract is a first-line marker + a parseable verdict, not a schema.

Its workflow gates merges by POSTing a commit status impl-review-ci/verdict to the PR HEAD and failing unless an impl-review-override label is present — concrete proof that the gate is a status check, with labels as trigger/override.
groundingSources.notes: Read in full 2026-06-09 (canonical source on this machine: /Users/psmyrdek/dev/10x-toolkit/...). The bundled workflow-template.yml stages the skill user-level (dodging the .claude-pr/ quarantine), runs claude-code-action@v1 --model claude-opus-4-7 --max-turns 60, validates [skip ci] + the output marker. Its actions/checkout@v4 is stale (now @v6) if shown verbatim. Learner prose must use the 10x-cli command, never the toolkit internal install.

newClaimsIntroduced: Composite Actions mechanics: action.yml with using: composite, inputs/outputs, github.action_path, per-step shell; separate-repo vs local-subfolder hosting; the consumer workflow shrinks to checkout + one uses: step.
Supply-chain rule: pin third-party actions to a commit SHA (@<sha>), not a moving tag (@v1), because a tag can be repointed at unverified code that runs with access to your secrets.

AI SDK v6 tool loop: tool() = description/inputSchema/execute; adding tools to the same ToolLoopAgent turns the scorer into a validate -> execute -> continue loop; stopWhen: stepCountIs caps it; isLoopFinished removes the cap (a CI cost risk); onStepFinish reports per-step token usage.
The read -> write -> whole-GitHub -> external 'ladder of agency' for adding tools.
OpenRouter as a single-key multi-vendor promptfoo provider (id: openrouter:<vendor>/<model>).
The 10x research -> plan -> implement flow seeded by a requirements.md brainstorm as the way to build a CI/CD feature (MVP review contract: input params, 1-10 criteria, ai-cr labels, retry label).
10x-impl-review-ci's two-layer structure (SKILL.md run-mechanics vs references/impl-review-instructions.md portable criteria).
claimsRemoved: Dropped vs the original spec/grounding: the dedicated 'DoD as a Zod schema' beat and the explicit 'required status check vs label' merge-gate teaching are NOT in the shipped draft. The draft mentions the merge gate only in passing (intro to sections 2-3) and structures output via Output.object inside the tool-loop section, deferring the status-check gate mechanics to the video scenario.
neighboringLessonReferencesChanged: None edited. m5-l2 is referenced as the artifact the CI calls and the ToolLoopAgent the tools attach to; m5-l4 boundary unchanged (standards/registry remain mustNotCover). Bridge-out to m5-l4 is implicit (Deep Dive forward-reference to extending the agent), not a hard setup.
potentialDuplicates: m5-l2: agent build, promptfoo as a concept, cost/privacy — fenced to referencesOnly (operationalized here, not re-introduced).
m5-l4: authoring/distributing team standards & shared registry — fenced to mustNotCover (standards appear only as the review criteria the agent enforces).
unsupportedFacts: Draft states the AI SDK default is 'stops after 20 steps (stopWhen: stepCountIs(20))'; verified against /vercel/ai @ ai_6.0.0-beta.128 that stepCountIs(20) is the canonical example, but the DEFAULT value when stopWhen is omitted was not confirmed from primary source. Re-verify at publish.
Deep Dive team-scaling framework (AI review as throughput not replacement; risk-based triage tiers) is a reasoned design framework, not measured industry consensus.
The override-rate metric and the 'what to log / where' collection sketch are a starter design, not a prescribed product.
The claude-code-action inline-comment buffering classifier model string is unpinned from primary source (present as 'a lightweight classifier').
promptfoo OpenRouter model IDs in the draft (claude-haiku-4.5, claude-sonnet-4.6, gpt-5-mini) are current-not-perennial; re-verify slugs at publish.
videoTextMismatches: The embedded course video shows the full merge-gate flow (ai-cr/verdict status check blocking/unblocking the merge), but the draft prose only mentions 'bramka, która zatrzyma merge' in passing and never teaches the status-check + gh-api mechanics. Intentional (gate lives in the video), but worth confirming the text isn't leaving an unpaid promise — see needsHumanDecision.
needsHumanDecision: RESOLVED 2026-06-14 (this backprop): the spec's provisional owns/referencesOnly/mustNotCover/learningOutcomes enrichment is now executed, derived from the SHIPPED DRAFT (not the stale spec proposal), per author confirmation.

RESOLVED at draft (author decision 2026-06-10): the AI SDK 6 / ToolLoopAgent build is the runnable CI example; validated end-to-end on przeprogramowani/10xCards (branch m5l3/ai-review-pipeline, PR #19).
RESOLVED at draft: the Zod divergence is EXPLAINED only (Deep Dive 'Dwie warstwy skilla 10x-impl-review-ci'); no port demonstrated.

OPEN: the draft defers the merge-gate mechanics (label as signal vs required status check posted via gh api) to the video and only alludes to the gate in prose. Decide whether the text should explicitly teach the status check or keep deferring to the video — flagged as a potential unpaid promise.

Toolkit wiring gap: 10x-toolkit course-content module-05/lesson-03.ts still carries the m5-l4 title ('Shared AI Registry') and wires no skills — the draft's 'npx @przeprogramowani/10x-cli@latest get m5l3' delivery claim requires wiring 10x-impl-review-ci to m5l3 before publish.

Draft pins promptfoo behavior verified on 0.120.15 (latest 0.121.x hard-requires Node >=22.22; TS provider hit ERR_UNKNOWN_FILE_EXTENSION on the tested version, .mjs provider used instead) — re-verify promptfoo version/Node guidance at publish.

lessons.title: Shared AI Registry: skille, komendy i reguły dla zespołu
lessons.titleEn: Shared AI Registry: skills, commands and rules for the team
owns: "AI artifacts are code" → distribution as an engineering problem (versioning, testing, distribution, clean uninstall)
The three-model distribution spectrum + audience-driven decision framework; GitHub Packages as the default recommendation
Read/write auth asymmetry as the single complexity illustration (write = free ephemeral token in CI; read = PAT plumbing everywhere)
Sentinel-marker pattern: distributing rules into files the user also edits; manifest-tracked install/update/uninstall
SKILL.md as cross-tool open standard → portability foundation
Plugin marketplaces (Claude Code, Cursor) as the single-tool alternative + tool-lock-in warning

The API+CLI model: audience argument (external/gated audience, diverse tech stacks, progressive delivery) and storage → gate → applicator architecture, with the transferable security trio (signing, sentinel-injection guard, API-host allowlist)
referencesOnly: konceptualny framing multi-repo — trzy warstwy (uniwersalny kontekst dla wszystkich repo; dystrybucja: jedno źródło prawdy + mechanizm paczka/CLI/MCP; widoczność cross-repo w runtime przez generowane kontrakty API i specyfikacje testów), odrzucenie centralnego meta-kontekstu oraz rozróżnienie „problemem nie jest duplikacja, tylko ręczne utrzymanie i synchronizacja" — m4-l1 Deep Dive „Multi-repo: wyzwania" (zakładane i referencowane wstecz, nie uczone od nowa)
m5-l3 review standards/DoD — the code-review skill appears only as example payload; its content stays shallow
MCP as alternative last mile to the same gate — one paragraph forward-pointer, not implemented here
prework [3.2] instruction hierarchy — assumed vocabulary
mustNotCover: Review-standards content depth (m5-l3) — the code-review skill stays a shallow example payload
MCP server implementation — MCP appears only as the "alternative last mile to the same gate" pointer
Internal toolkit commands in learner-facing prose (learners see 10x auth / 10x get only)
Teaching cloud/IaC from scratch — Terraform/IAM/AWS surface as concrete gotchas and cost lines, not a beginner's AWS tutorial
learningOutcomes: Learner can name the requirements an AI-artifact distribution mechanism must meet (one source of truth, versioning, auth, install/update/uninstall, multi-tool delivery).
Learner can pick a distribution model for their org using the audience-driven decision framework and justify the choice.
Learner understands the shape of the GitHub Packages setup (publishConfig, scope mapping, read/write token split) and where its real complexity lives.
Learner recognizes when an audience (external, gated, revocable) forces the API+CLI model and can sketch its three layers.
Learner can take the spec handout and bootstrap an equivalent pipeline for their own team with AI.

groundingSources.title: Working with the npm registry — GitHub Packages
groundingSources.relevance: Model 2 mechanics: publishConfig + .npmrc scope mapping, GITHUB_TOKEN publishing, and the classic-PAT-only read auth limitation
claimsSupported: GitHub Packages only supports authentication using a personal access token (classic); fine-grained PATs are not supported
Publishing requires only publishConfig.registry in package.json or an .npmrc scope mapping
GITHUB_TOKEN publishes packages associated with the workflow repository in Actions
groundingSources.notes: Most aging-prone claim of the lesson — pair with roadmap #558 and re-check at RC review; use 'as of mid-2026' framing in prose

groundingSources.title: About billing for GitHub Packages
groundingSources.relevance: Decision-table cost row for model 2
claimsSupported: GitHub Packages is free for public packages and includes private-package storage/transfer quota in every GitHub plan (shared with Actions)
groundingSources.notes: Do not hard-code GB quota numbers in prose; link the page instead

groundingSources.title: Packages support for fine-grained PATs (roadmap #558)
groundingSources.relevance: Justifies 'as of' framing on the classic-PAT limitation
claimsSupported: Fine-grained PAT support for GitHub Packages is an acknowledged roadmap item, open since September 2022 and still open as of 2026-06-04
groundingSources.notes: Re-check immediately before publication; if shipped, the asymmetry beat survives but the 'classic only' sentence must change

groundingSources.title: AWS CodeArtifact authentication and tokens
groundingSources.relevance: Model 1 cost line: token lifetime
claimsSupported: CodeArtifact authorization tokens are valid for a default period of 12 hours (configurable 15 minutes to 12 hours)

groundingSources.title: Agent Skills — open standard
groundingSources.relevance: Portability foundation beat: SKILL.md as cross-tool open standard; Materiały dodatkowe
claimsSupported: SKILL.md was originally developed by Anthropic and released as an open standard
Adopters as of 2026-06-04 include Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Cursor, VS Code, GitHub Copilot, Gemini CLI, Goose, OpenCode, Amp and more
groundingSources.notes: Name 4-6 adopters max from the current showcase; do not claim ChatGPT (not on current client list)

groundingSources.title: Create and distribute a plugin marketplace — Claude Code docs
groundingSources.relevance: Beat 9 alternative: zero-infra single-tool distribution with real team mechanics
claimsSupported: A marketplace is a marketplace.json catalog in a git repo; users add it via /plugin marketplace add
Teams can auto-prompt install via extraKnownMarketplaces + enabledPlugins in .claude/settings.json
Organizations can restrict allowed marketplaces via strictKnownMarketplaces in managed settings
groundingSources.notes: Stronger team story than the Feb 2026 webinar research recorded — present as viable single-tool path, lock-in is the channel not the artifact

groundingSources.title: Cursor Marketplace
groundingSources.relevance: Beat 9: Cursor's official marketplace resolves the webinar-era open question
claimsSupported: Cursor operates an official marketplace distributing plugins (MCP servers + agent skills) and automations
groundingSources.notes: Landing-page evidence; team-distribution mechanics undocumented there — don't claim parity with Claude Code team settings

groundingSources.title: Claude Skills are awesome, maybe a bigger deal than MCP
groundingSources.relevance: MCP forward-pointer paragraph: file delivery vs protocol for static artifacts
claimsSupported: Skills are portable markdown files usable across tools without protocol implementation
MCP servers consume tens of thousands of context tokens while skills cost dozens until activated
groundingSources.notes: Credible practitioner opinion — use for framing, not benchmark-grade facts; don't import MCP-vs-skills rivalry tone

groundingSources.title: Hacker News: Claude Skills launch discussion
groundingSources.relevance: Practitioner pains and objections shaping beats 1 and 9
claimsSupported: Practitioners voice demand for org-wide skill sharing and package-manager-like workflows ('skill add foo')
Recurring skepticism to disarm: skills as 'just prompts in files', 'how is this different from MCP prompts?'

groundingSources.title: Internal digest: webinar build — CodeArtifact + Terraform (model 1)
groundingSources.relevance: Model 1 section: pipeline anatomy, infra cost lines, gotchas, spec-driven build + handout specs
claimsSupported: Full registry-as-infrastructure pipeline anatomy (skill, pack, CodeArtifact, CI/CD, install) and its real costs
CodeArtifact-vs-Verdaccio decision rationale; spec-driven AI build workflow

groundingSources.title: Internal digest: GitHub Packages production system (model 2)
groundingSources.relevance: Model 2 section: read/write token asymmetry, auth plumbing as code, sentinel markers, release engineering
claimsSupported: Read/write token split in one CI job (read via long-lived PAT, write via ephemeral GITHUB_TOKEN)
Sentinel-marker pattern for distributing rules into user-edited files; manifest-tracked install/uninstall
groundingSources.notes: Author-progression narrative (AWS webinar build to GH Packages production) is an author decision confirmed 2026-06-04, not a sourced external fact

groundingSources.title: Internal digest: API + CLI delivery model (model 3)
groundingSources.relevance: Model 3 section: audience argument, storage-gate-applicator architecture, the 10x get reveal, decision table rows

Three-layer architecture: R2 storage, Worker gate, CLI applicator; MCP as alternative last mile to the same gate
